Activity 1: What is "Tell" vs "Show"?
options: a) Tom was happy.
b) Tom smiled from ear to ear and jumped in the air.
c) Tom laughed loudly and clapped his hands together.
questionJson.correct: a) Tom was happy.

answerJson.correct: a) Tom was happy.

questionJson.question: Activity 2: Identify the SHOWING Sentence
options: a) The house was old.
b) Paint peeled from the walls and the wooden steps creaked with every footstep.
c) It was a very old building.
questionJson.correct: b) Paint peeled from the walls and the wooden steps creaked with every footstep.

answerJson.correct: b) Paint peeled from the walls and the wooden steps creaked with every footstep.

questionJson.question: Activity 3: The Movie Test
options: a) Jake felt nervous.
b) The situation was scary.
c) Jake's hands trembled as he wiped the sweat from his forehead.
questionJson.correct: c) Jake's hands trembled as he wiped the sweat from his forehead.

answerJson.correct: c) Jake's hands trembled as he wiped the sweat from his forehead.

questionJson.question: Activity 4: Transform TELL to SHOW
options: a) The dog was very happy and excited.
b) The dog wagged its tail rapidly and jumped up at its owner.
c) The dog felt really excited about seeing its owner.
questionJson.correct: b) The dog wagged its tail rapidly and jumped up at its owner.

answerJson.correct: b) The dog wagged its tail rapidly and jumped up at its owner.

questionJson.question: Activity 5: Show Through ACTION
options: a) Emma was very tired after the long day.
b) Emma yawned three times and rubbed her eyes as she slumped into the chair.
c) Emma felt exhausted and tired.
questionJson.correct: b) Emma yawned three times and rubbed her eyes as she slumped into the chair.

answerJson.correct: b) Emma yawned three times and rubbed her eyes as she slumped into the chair.

questionJson.question: Activity 6: Show Through DIALOGUE
options: a) Mum was very proud and happy.
b) "I'm so proud of you!" Mum beamed, pulling me into a warm hug.
c) Mum felt proud when she saw my work.
questionJson.correct: b) "I'm so proud of you!" Mum beamed, pulling me into a warm hug.

answerJson.correct: b) "I'm so proud of you!" Mum beamed, pulling me into a warm hug.

questionJson.question: Activity 7: The Alfie Example
options: a) It's longer
b) It gives specific examples of his happy behavior that we can picture
c) It uses difficult words
questionJson.correct: b) It gives specific examples of his happy behavior that we can picture

answerJson.correct: b) It gives specific examples of his happy behavior that we can picture

questionJson.question: Activity 8: Using the 6 Sensory Questions
options: a) The bakery was really nice and pleasant.
b) The smell of fresh bread filled the air. Warm croissants sat in the window and the sound of the oven timer dinged every few minutes.
c) It was a good bakery with nice things.
questionJson.correct: b) The smell of fresh bread filled the air. Warm croissants sat in the window and the sound of the oven timer dinged every few minutes.

answerJson.correct: b) The smell of fresh bread filled the air. Warm croissants sat in the window and the sound of the oven timer dinged every few minutes.

questionJson.question: Activity 9: Multiple Sentences for Showing
options: a) The storm was very frightening and scary.
b) Lightning split the dark sky. Thunder crashed so loudly that the windows rattled. Rain hammered against the roof like a thousand tiny drums.
c) It was a frightening storm that scared everyone.
questionJson.correct: b) Lightning split the dark sky. Thunder crashed so loudly that the windows rattled. Rain hammered against the roof like a thousand tiny drums.

answerJson.correct: b) Lightning split the dark sky. Thunder crashed so loudly that the windows rattled. Rain hammered against the roof like a thousand tiny drums.

questionJson.question: Activity 10: Apply the Movie Test
options: a) I felt very scared as I walked home.
b) My heart pounded. I glanced over my shoulder every few steps. Every shadow seemed to move.
c) Walking home was a scary experience for me.
questionJson.correct: b) My heart pounded. I glanced over my shoulder every few steps. Every shadow seemed to move.

answerJson.correct: b) My heart pounded. I glanced over my shoulder every few steps. Every shadow seemed to move.

b) "No talking!" Mrs. Davies snapped, her eyes scanning the silent classroom. "If I see one pencil down, the whole class stays at break."
c) My teacher was known for being strict.
questionJson.correct: b) "No talking!" Mrs. Davies snapped, her eyes scanning the silent classroom. "If I see one pencil down, the whole class stays at break."

answerJson.correct: b) "No talking!" Mrs. Davies snapped, her eyes scanning the silent classroom. "If I see one pencil down, the whole class stays at break."

questionJson.correct: b) Children raced across the grass. Dogs barked at the ice cream van. Three families competed for the last free bench.

answerJson.correct: b) Children raced across the grass. Dogs barked at the ice cream van. Three families competed for the last free bench.

options: a) Notes: SEE-colorful stalls, HEAR-vendors shouting prices, SMELL-spices & fish, TOUCH-bumping through crowd, TASTE-sample fruits, FEEL-excited & overwhelmed

questionJson.question: Activity 18: Using ALL Your Skills
options: a) Grandma was a very wise and clever person.

b) Grandma listened carefully before speaking. "Life is like a river," she said quietly, her wrinkled hands folding gently in her lap. "Sometimes you must flow with it, sometimes you must swim against it."
c) Everyone said Grandma was wise and gave good advice.
questionJson.correct: b) Grandma listened carefully before speaking. "Life is like a river," she said quietly, her wrinkled hands folding gently in her lap. "Sometimes you must flow with it, sometimes you must swim against it."

answerJson.correct: b) Grandma listened carefully before speaking. "Life is like a river," she said quietly, her wrinkled hands folding gently in her lap. "Sometimes you must flow with it, sometimes you must swim against it."

questionJson.question: Activity 19: Complex Showing in Your Writing
options: a) The first day was really hard and difficult for me.

b) My stomach twisted into knots as I pushed open the heavy door. Hundreds of unfamiliar faces stared. I clutched my bag tighter, desperately searching for a friendly smile. "You're in the wrong corridor," someone muttered. My cheeks burned as hot as fire.
c) I found the first day at my new school to be very difficult and challenging.
questionJson.correct: b) My stomach twisted into knots as I pushed open the heavy door. Hundreds of unfamiliar faces stared. I clutched my bag tighter, desperately searching for a friendly smile. "You're in the wrong corridor," someone muttered. My cheeks burned as hot as fire.

answerJson.correct: b) My stomach twisted into knots as I pushed open the heavy door. Hundreds of unfamiliar faces stared. I clutched my bag tighter, desperately searching for a friendly smile. "You're in the wrong corridor," someone muttered. My cheeks burned as hot as fire.

questionJson.question: Activity 20: Your Fifth Draft Challenge
options: a) Write lots of new sentences immediately
b) Make editorial notes with key points about what to show (using Movie Test and sensory questions)
c) Delete all the tell statements
questionJson.correct: b) Make editorial notes with key points about what to show (using Movie Test and sensory questions)

answerJson.correct: b) Make editorial notes with key points about what to show (using Movie Test and sensory questions)

questionJson.question: Activity 1: Why Read Aloud?
options: a) A sentence that doesn't make sense or sounds awkward
b) Your name at the top of the page
c) The date you wrote it
questionJson.correct: a) A sentence that doesn't make sense or sounds awkward

answerJson.correct: a) A sentence that doesn't make sense or sounds awkward

questionJson.question: Activity 2: The Kind, Specific, Helpful Framework
options: a) "This is rubbish."
b) "I love your opening sentence! In paragraph 2, could you add more detail about what the character looks like?"
c) "It's okay I guess."
questionJson.correct: b) "I love your opening sentence! In paragraph 2, could you add more detail about what the character looks like?"

answerJson.correct: b) "I love your opening sentence! In paragraph 2, could you add more detail about what the character looks like?"

questionJson.question: Activity 3: What Makes Feedback KIND?
options: a) "You made so many mistakes!"
b) "I can see you worked really hard on this. Let me show you how to fix a few things."
c) "This doesn't make any sense."
questionJson.correct: b) "I can see you worked really hard on this. Let me show you how to fix a few things."

answerJson.correct: b) "I can see you worked really hard on this. Let me show you how to fix a few things."

questionJson.question: Activity 4: SPECIFIC vs VAGUE Feedback
options: a) "Add more description."
b) "In paragraph 2, can you describe what the house looks like? I can't picture it."
c) "It needs improvement."
questionJson.correct: b) "In paragraph 2, can you describe what the house looks like? I can't picture it."

answerJson.correct: b) "In paragraph 2, can you describe what the house looks like? I can't picture it."

questionJson.question: Activity 5: HELPFUL Suggestions
options: a) "The ending is bad."
b) "The ending works, but you could make it stronger by showing the character's reaction."
c) "I don't like it."
questionJson.correct: b) "The ending works, but you could make it stronger by showing the character's reaction."

answerJson.correct: b) "The ending works, but you could make it stronger by showing the character's reaction."

questionJson.question: Activity 6: Complete Feedback Example
options: a) "Great story idea! In paragraph 3, I got confused about who was speaking. Could you add 'said Tom' after the dialogue?"
b) "You forgot something in paragraph 3."
c) "This is confusing."
questionJson.correct: a) "Great story idea! In paragraph 3, I got confused about who was speaking. Could you add 'said Tom' after the dialogue?"

answerJson.correct: a) "Great story idea! In paragraph 3, I got confused about who was speaking. Could you add 'said Tom' after the dialogue?"

questionJson.question: Activity 7: Reading with Expression
options: a) After every word
b) At punctuation marks like commas and full stops
c) Never pause
questionJson.correct: b) At punctuation marks like commas and full stops

answerJson.correct: b) At punctuation marks like commas and full stops

questionJson.question: Activity 8: Catching Errors by Ear
options: a) No errors
b) "The" is repeated, and "the" is missing before "park" and "fence"
c) The sentence is too short
questionJson.correct: b) "The" is repeated, and "the" is missing before "park" and "fence"

answerJson.correct: b) "The" is repeated, and "the" is missing before "park" and "fence"

questionJson.question: Activity 9: Listening for Flow
options: a) The sentence is perfect
b) Too many "and I" - it sounds choppy and repetitive
c) Not enough detail
questionJson.correct: b) Too many "and I" - it sounds choppy and repetitive

answerJson.correct: b) Too many "and I" - it sounds choppy and repetitive

questionJson.question: Activity 10: Start with Something Positive
options: a) "The ending is terrible!"
b) "Your opening grabbed my attention! The ending could be stronger if you showed what happened to the character. I think this could be a really exciting story!"
c) "It's okay."
questionJson.correct: b) "Your opening grabbed my attention! The ending could be stronger if you showed what happened to the character. I think this could be a really exciting story!"

answerJson.correct: b) "Your opening grabbed my attention! The ending could be stronger if you showed what happened to the character. I think this could be a really exciting story!"

questionJson.question: Activity 11: Be Specific About Location
options: a) "Something is wrong somewhere."
b) "In the second paragraph, third sentence, you wrote 'there' but I think you meant 'their'."
c) "Check your spelling."
questionJson.correct: b) "In the second paragraph, third sentence, you wrote 'there' but I think you meant 'their'."

answerJson.correct: b) "In the second paragraph, third sentence, you wrote 'there' but I think you meant 'their'."

questionJson.question: Activity 12: Focus on One or Two Things
options: a) Tell them all 5 problems at once
b) Pick the 1-2 most important things and give specific, helpful feedback on those
c) Don't say anything
questionJson.correct: b) Pick the 1-2 most important things and give specific, helpful feedback on those

answerJson.correct: b) Pick the 1-2 most important things and give specific, helpful feedback on those

questionJson.question: Activity 13: Ask Questions Instead of Telling
options: a) "You didn't describe the setting."
b) "What did the forest look like? Could you add some description so I can picture it?"
c) "This needs more detail."
questionJson.correct: b) "What did the forest look like? Could you add some description so I can picture it?"

answerJson.correct: b) "What did the forest look like? Could you add some description so I can picture it?"

questionJson.question: Activity 14: Listen Without Interrupting
options: a) Interrupt to explain why you wrote it that way
b) Listen quietly, take notes, say thank you, then decide what to change
c) Argue that your writing is perfect
questionJson.correct: b) Listen quietly, take notes, say thank you, then decide what to change

answerJson.correct: b) Listen quietly, take notes, say thank you, then decide what to change

questionJson.question: Activity 15: Say Thank You
options: a) "That's a terrible idea!"
b) "Thank you for the suggestion! Let me think about that."
c) "You don't understand my story."
questionJson.correct: b) "Thank you for the suggestion! Let me think about that."

answerJson.correct: b) "Thank you for the suggestion! Let me think about that."

questionJson.question: Activity 16: You Choose What to Change
options: a) Change everything they suggested immediately
b) Think about each suggestion and use the ones that will make your story better
c) Ignore all feedback because it's your story
questionJson.correct: b) Think about each suggestion and use the ones that will make your story better

answerJson.correct: b) Think about each suggestion and use the ones that will make your story better

questionJson.question: Activity 17: Ask for Clarification
options: a) "Which part of the middle? Can you show me the exact sentence?"
b) Nothing - just pretend you understand
c) "You're wrong!"
questionJson.correct: a) "Which part of the middle? Can you show me the exact sentence?"

answerJson.correct: a) "Which part of the middle? Can you show me the exact sentence?"

questionJson.question: Activity 18: Complete Feedback Example
options: a) "This is boring and too short."

b) "Nice start! Your sentences all begin with 'I went' which makes it sound a bit choppy. Could you try varying how your sentences start? For example: 'I went to the beach. The sun was hot. Swimming in the cool water felt amazing!' This would make it more interesting to read."
c) "Write more."
questionJson.correct: b) "Nice start! Your sentences all begin with 'I went' which makes it sound a bit choppy. Could you try varying how your sentences start? For example: 'I went to the beach. The sun was hot. Swimming in the cool water felt amazing!' This would make it more interesting to read."

answerJson.correct: b) "Nice start! Your sentences all begin with 'I went' which makes it sound a bit choppy. Could you try varying how your sentences start? For example: 'I went to the beach. The sun was hot. Swimming in the cool water felt amazing!' This would make it more interesting to read."

questionJson.question: Activity 19: Peer Feedback Checklist
options: a) To make feedback take longer
b) To ensure feedback is helpful and doesn't hurt the writer's feelings
c) Teachers require it
questionJson.correct: b) To ensure feedback is helpful and doesn't hurt the writer's feelings

answerJson.correct: b) To ensure feedback is helpful and doesn't hurt the writer's feelings

questionJson.question: Activity 20: Your Reading and Feedback Plan
options: a) Read through your writing silently
b) Give your partner feedback
c) Make all the changes immediately
questionJson.correct: a) Read through your writing silently

answerJson.correct: a) Read through your writing silently

questionJson.question: Activity 1: What is Cohesion?
options: a) "I went to school. I like ice cream. My cat is fluffy."
b) "I went to school. In my first lesson, we learned about planets. After lunch, we went outside to play."
c) "The end. Once upon a time. They lived happily."
questionJson.correct: b) "I went to school. In my first lesson, we learned about planets. After lunch, we went outside to play."

answerJson.correct: b) "I went to school. In my first lesson, we learned about planets. After lunch, we went outside to play."

questionJson.question: Activity 2: Why Cohesion Matters
options: a) Too many characters
b) Events jump around - how did Tom get to the beach? Where did the dragon come from?
c) The sentences are too short
questionJson.correct: b) Events jump around - how did Tom get to the beach? Where did the dragon come from?

answerJson.correct: b) Events jump around - how did Tom get to the beach? Where did the dragon come from?

questionJson.question: Activity 3: The Five-Part Story Timeline
options: a) It makes the story longer
b) It shows the order of events - we can spot gaps where events jump
c) It tells us which words to use
questionJson.correct: b) It shows the order of events - we can spot gaps where events jump

answerJson.correct: b) It shows the order of events - we can spot gaps where events jump

questionJson.question: Activity 4: Spotting the Gap
options: a) What Sarah looks like
b) What happened between Monday and Friday to earn the trophy
c) Where the school is located
questionJson.correct: b) What happened between Monday and Friday to earn the trophy

answerJson.correct: b) What happened between Monday and Friday to earn the trophy

questionJson.question: Activity 5: Timeline Shows Gaps
options: a) Only the Opening
b) Build-up (how he got lost) and Resolution (how he was found)
c) Only the Ending
questionJson.correct: b) Build-up (how he got lost) and Resolution (how he was found)

answerJson.correct: b) Build-up (how he got lost) and Resolution (how he was found)

questionJson.question: Activity 6: Question to Find Gaps
options: a) "What did Emma eat?"
b) "What happened between breakfast and dinner to make her cry?"
c) "What color is Emma's hair?"
questionJson.correct: b) "What happened between breakfast and dinner to make her cry?"

answerJson.correct: b) "What happened between breakfast and dinner to make her cry?"

questionJson.question: Activity 7: What are Transitions?
options: a) "Tom played football. Tom went home."
b) "Tom played football. After the game, he went home tired but happy."
c) "Tom. Football. Home."
questionJson.correct: b) "Tom played football. After the game, he went home tired but happy."

answerJson.correct: b) "Tom played football. After the game, he went home tired but happy."

questionJson.question: Activity 8: Time Transitions
options: a) "Also, the cat was hungry."
b) "Three hours later, the rescue team arrived."
c) "The big red house."
questionJson.correct: b) "Three hours later, the rescue team arrived."

answerJson.correct: b) "Three hours later, the rescue team arrived."

questionJson.question: Activity 9: Adding a Transition
options: a) "Because"
b) "Finally"
c) "However"
questionJson.correct: b) "Finally"

answerJson.correct: b) "Finally"

questionJson.question: Activity 10: Planning to Fill Gaps
options: a) "Ben had a pet dog."
b) "For weeks, Ben practiced his speech every evening after school."
c) "Ben won the trophy."
questionJson.correct: b) "For weeks, Ben practiced his speech every evening after school."

answerJson.correct: b) "For weeks, Ben practiced his speech every evening after school."

questionJson.question: Activity 11: Logical Order
options: a) A → B → C
b) B → C → A

questionJson.question: What is a news report?

feedback.correct: ✅ Correct! A news report is a factual text. It tells readers about something real that has happened.
feedback.wrong: A news report is factual, not made-up. It tells readers about a real event that has happened.

questionJson.question:
Look at this headline:
"Pupils Raise £500 for Animal Shelter at Charity Fun Run"

What does a headline do?

feedback.correct: ✅ Correct! A headline gives the most important facts quickly and clearly so readers know what the story is about.
feedback.wrong: A headline captures the key facts of the story at a glance. It should be short and clear.

questionJson.question:
Read this opening paragraph:

"Oakwood Primary School hosted its annual charity fun run on Friday 15th June, with over 200 pupils completing the five-kilometre course around the school grounds. The event raised £500 for the local animal shelter."

Which word describes the TONE of this paragraph?

feedback.correct: ✅ Correct! News reports use an objective tone – the writer reports the facts without adding their own feelings or opinions.
feedback.wrong: News reports use an objective tone. The writer reports facts without adding personal feelings. Look at the extract – it simply states what happened.

questionJson.question: The "5Ws and H" are the key questions a news reporter must answer. Which of these is NOT one of the 5Ws and H?

feedback.correct: ✅ Correct! "What did the characters feel?" belongs in a story, not a news report. News reporters answer: Who, What, When, Where, Why, and How.
feedback.wrong: The 5Ws and H are: Who, What, When, Where, Why, and How. Feelings belong in stories. News reports stick to facts.

questionJson.question:
Read this sentence:

"I thought it looked different from ordinary rocks," Emily recalled.

What is this type of sentence called in a news report?

feedback.correct: ✅ Correct! This is a direct quote. The journalist uses the exact words a person said, inside quotation marks, to add authenticity to the report.
feedback.wrong: When a journalist uses someone\'s exact words inside speech marks, it is called a direct quote. Quotes make a report more believable.

questionJson.question: A news report uses the "inverted pyramid" structure. What does this mean?

feedback.correct: ✅ Correct! The inverted pyramid means the most important information comes FIRST. Readers get the key facts straight away, even if they only read the first paragraph.
feedback.wrong: The inverted pyramid means the MOST important facts come first. Then supporting details. Then background. This way, readers get the key information immediately.

questionJson.question: Match each 5W/H question to the correct answer from the fun run news report.
questionJson.instruction: Match each item on the left to its pair on the right.

questionJson.question: These four sentences are from a news report. Which one should come FIRST in the report?

feedback.correct: ✅ Correct! The opening sentence (called the "lead") must give the most important facts: who, what, when, and the key outcome. The other sentences belong further down the report.
feedback.wrong: The opening sentence gives the most important facts immediately. Option C tells us who (Oakwood Primary), what (fun run), when (15th June), and the key outcome (£500 raised). The others are supporting details.

questionJson.question: Which of these sentences uses the correct TENSE for a news report about an event that has already happened?

feedback.correct: ✅ Correct! News reports about past events use the past tense. "Ran" and "cheered" tell us the event has already happened.
feedback.wrong: News reports about past events use the past tense. Option B uses "ran" and "cheered" – both past tense verbs. The others use present, future, or imperative forms.

questionJson.question: Match each news report feature to its correct definition.
questionJson.instruction: Match each item on the left to its pair on the right.

questionJson.question: Complete the lead paragraph using the word bank. Choose the correct word for each gap.
questionJson.template: Complete the lead paragraph using the word bank. Choose the correct word for each gap.
questionJson.feedback: ✅ Well done! You completed the lead paragraph correctly. Notice how it answers: WHO (a twelve-year-old girl), WHAT (discovered a footprint), WHERE (Brighton), and WHEN (last Saturday).

questionJson.question: A journalist wants to include a quote from the headteacher. Which version is written correctly for a news report?

feedback.correct: ✅ Correct! The quote is inside speech marks, a comma comes before the closing speech mark, and the reporting clause (Councillor Hughes stated) follows. Capital letters are used correctly.
feedback.wrong: Option A is correct. The spoken words go inside speech marks. A comma comes before the closing speech mark. The speaker\'s name and a reporting verb follow. Both use capital letters correctly.

questionJson.question:
Read this quote from the football report:

"I could not believe it when the ball went in," eleven-year-old Jessica said afterwards.

What is the PURPOSE of including a quote in a news report?

feedback.correct: ✅ Correct! Quotes add authenticity – they show readers that the journalist spoke to real people. They also give a human, personal perspective on the facts.
feedback.wrong: Quotes add authenticity and a human perspective. They show the journalist spoke directly to people involved. They should add to the facts, not replace them.

questionJson.question: Complete this headline using the most precise and factual language.
questionJson.template: Complete this headline using the most precise and factual language.
questionJson.feedback: ✅ Correct! "Wins" is a precise, factual verb. Headlines use short, active verbs to describe exactly what happened.

questionJson.question: A pupil has written this lead paragraph. What is the MAIN problem with it?\n\n"The football match was amazing! Millbrook Primary played really well and the crowd went absolutely crazy when they scored at the last second. It was incredible to watch."

feedback.correct: ✅ Correct! "Amazing", "really well", "went absolutely crazy", and "incredible" are all opinions and emotional language. A news report must be objective – it reports facts without the writer\'s feelings.
feedback.wrong: The main problem is opinion and emotional language. Words like "amazing", "really well", and "incredible" show the writer\'s feelings. News reports must be objective and factual.

questionJson.question: Write a lead paragraph for this news story.
questionJson.prompt: Write a lead paragraph for this news story.

questionJson.question: A journalist has four quotes to include in a news report about a new school library. Which quote should come FIRST?

feedback.correct: ✅ Correct! The councillor\'s quote provides key factual information – the funding amount and the campaign behind the project. Important facts from authoritative sources come before personal reactions.
feedback.wrong: The councillor\'s quote gives the most important factual information: the funding amount (£15,000) and how it was achieved. Authoritative, factual quotes come before personal reactions from pupils.

questionJson.question: Read this news report extract and use the checklist to evaluate it.

A pupil is writing a news report about their school winning a gardening competition. They have written the headline:

"Our School is the Best at Gardening and We Are So Proud!"

Rewrite the headline in your head, then choose the BEST improved version.

feedback.correct: ✅ Correct! Option B is factual, objective, and includes the school name and the key outcome. It avoids opinion ("best", "amazing") and uses a clear, active structure.
feedback.wrong: Option B is best. It names the school, states the result clearly, and avoids opinion language. Headlines must be factual and objective, never boastful or emotional.

questionJson.question: Progressive Writing Practice (PWP)
What is an information report?

feedback.correct: ✅ Correct! An information report is a factual text. It describes and explains a topic using organised sections and accurate facts.
feedback.wrong: An information report is factual and organised. It explains a topic clearly in sections. It does not tell a story, persuade, or describe personal events.

questionJson.question:
Read this opening sentence:

"The honey bee is one of the most important insects in the world."

What is the purpose of this sentence in an information report?

feedback.correct: ✅ Correct! This is a topic sentence – it introduces the subject (honey bee) and signals its importance. Information reports begin by defining the topic clearly.
feedback.wrong: This is a topic sentence. It introduces the subject and its significance. Information reports do not have headlines or personal opinions, and they do not open with quotes.

questionJson.question:
Look at these two sentences:

A: "The water cycle is the most fascinating thing I have ever studied."
B: "The water cycle describes the continuous movement of water on, above, and below Earth's surface."

Which sentence is written in the style of an information report?

feedback.correct: ✅ Correct! Sentence B uses precise, objective language to define the topic. Information reports describe facts without the writer\'s personal feelings or opinions.
feedback.wrong: Sentence B is correct. It defines the topic with clear, factual language. Sentence A uses personal opinion ("most fascinating") which does not belong in an information report.

questionJson.question: Information reports and news reports are both factual. What is the KEY difference between them?

feedback.correct: ✅ Correct! This is the critical distinction. News reports: recent event, past tense, inverted pyramid. Information reports: any topic, present tense for facts, subheadings to organise sections.
feedback.wrong: The key difference is structure and tense. News reports describe recent events in past tense using the inverted pyramid. Information reports explain topics using subheadings and present tense for ongoing facts.

questionJson.question:
Read this extract from the honey bee report:

"Adult honey bees measure approximately 12 to 15 millimetres in length. Their bodies are covered in fine hairs and divided into three distinct parts: the head, thorax, and abdomen."

What is the subheading for this section most likely to be?

feedback.correct: ✅ Correct! This section describes the bee\'s physical features – size, body covering, and body parts. "Physical Characteristics" is the precise, appropriate subheading.
feedback.wrong: "Physical Characteristics" is correct. The section describes measurements, body covering, and body parts. Subheadings must accurately summarise the content of the section beneath them.

questionJson.question:
The Victorian Railways model text uses TWO different tenses. Read these sentences and identify which is correct:

"Steam locomotives burned coal to heat water in a boiler." (past tense)
"Trains transport millions of passengers every year." (present tense)

Why does an information report sometimes use BOTH tenses?

feedback.correct: ✅ Correct! Present tense is used for ongoing truths ("bees are pollinators"). Past tense is used for completed historical events ("Stephenson designed the Rocket in 1829"). This distinction shows careful, precise writing.
feedback.wrong: Tense is deliberate in information reports. Present tense = facts still true now. Past tense = historical events that happened and are finished. Both can appear in the same report on a historical topic.

questionJson.question: Match each subheading to the correct section summary from the honey bee report.
questionJson.instruction: Match each item on the left to its pair on the right.

questionJson.question: A pupil is writing an information report about dolphins. They have four section headings. Which list is in the BEST order for an information report?

feedback.correct: ✅ Correct! Information reports follow a logical order: Introduction (what/why) → organised information sections (what it looks like, where it lives, how it behaves) → Conclusion (significance, future). This guides the reader clearly.
feedback.wrong: The correct order is: Introduction → Physical Characteristics → Habitat and Behaviour → Conservation. This moves from general (what it is) to specific (what it does) to broader context (why it matters).

questionJson.question:
Read these two sentences:

A: "The water cycle is amazing and extremely cool."
B: "The water cycle distributes fresh water across continents, enabling organisms to survive far from oceans."

Sentence B is better for an information report. Which THREE features make it better?

feedback.correct: ✅ Correct! The three features that make Sentence B better are: precise verb ("distributes"), specific detail ("across continents"), and objective language (no "amazing" or "cool"). These are three hallmarks of strong information report writing.
feedback.wrong: Sentence B is better because it uses a precise verb ("distributes"), includes specific information ("across continents, enabling organisms to survive"), and avoids opinion words. No quotes or statistics are needed.

questionJson.question: Match each information report feature to its correct definition.
questionJson.instruction: Match each item on the left to its pair on the right.

questionJson.question: Complete this topic sentence from the solar system report using the word bank.
questionJson.template: Complete this topic sentence from the solar system report using the word bank.
questionJson.feedback: ✅ Well done! You used precise vocabulary: "comprises" (made up of), "orbits" (moves around), and "formed" (past tense for a historical event). Notice "formed" is past tense because formation is a completed event.

questionJson.question:
A pupil writes this sentence in their honey bee report:

"Honey bees are really, really important for pollinating plants and without them we would be in big trouble."

How should they rewrite it for an information report?

feedback.correct: ✅ Correct! Option A uses precise vocabulary ("essential pollinators", "fertilising"), includes a specific statistic ("one-third"), and avoids all opinion language. This is exactly the standard for information report writing.
feedback.wrong: Option A is correct. It replaces vague, informal language ("really, really important", "big trouble") with precise vocabulary ("essential pollinators", "fertilising") and a specific statistic ("one-third of all crops").

questionJson.question:
Read this passage from the water cycle report:

"As water vapour rises high into the atmosphere, it encounters cooler temperatures. When the vapour cools sufficiently, it condenses back into tiny liquid water droplets or ice crystals."

What word is defined IN CONTEXT in the second sentence?

feedback.correct: ✅ Correct! "Condenses" is the technical term being defined in context. The writer does not stop to give a separate definition – instead they explain the process immediately after using the word. This is a hallmark of skilled information report writing.
feedback.wrong: "Condenses" is the technical term. The writer explains what condensing means immediately: "cools… back into tiny liquid water droplets or ice crystals." Defining technical terms within the text keeps reading flowing smoothly.

questionJson.question: Complete this sentence from the Victorian Railways report. Use the correct tense for each gap.
questionJson.template: Complete this sentence from the Victorian Railways report. Use the correct tense for each gap.
questionJson.feedback: ✅ Correct! "Opened" is past tense because the railway\'s opening in 1825 is a completed historical event. "Connects" is present tense because the rail network still exists and functions today. Using both tenses correctly shows precision.

questionJson.question:
A pupil has written this section of an information report about Jupiter:

"Jupiter is a planet. It is very big. It has a storm on it. The storm is called the Great Red Spot. It has been there for a long time. Jupiter has lots of moons."

What is the MAIN problem with this writing?

feedback.correct: ✅ Correct! The writing lacks precision. Every sentence is short and vague: "very big" instead of "could contain 1,300 Earths"; "a long time" instead of "centuries"; "lots of moons" instead of "at least 79 moons". Information reports need specific data and technical vocabulary.
feedback.wrong: The main problem is lack of precision. "Very big" should be "could contain 1,300 Earths". "A long time" should be "for centuries". "Lots of moons" should be "at least 79 moons". Information reports require specific data and vocabulary.

questionJson.question: Write a topic sentence for a new section called "Diet and Feeding" in an information report about dolphins.
questionJson.prompt: Write a topic sentence for a new section called "Diet and Feeding" in an information report about dolphins.

questionJson.question: A pupil is planning an information report about volcanoes. They have collected these facts. Which fact should go in the section "How Volcanoes Erupt"?

feedback.correct: ✅ Correct! Option B explains the process by which volcanoes erupt – magma, pressure, tectonic plates, lava. This is a process explanation that belongs in "How Volcanoes Erupt". The other facts belong in Introduction, Etymology, or Impact sections.
feedback.wrong: Option B is correct. It explains the eruption process: magma, pressure, tectonic plates, lava reaching the surface. This is process information. The other facts belong in different sections: statistics (Intro), word origins (Etymology), farming (Impact on Humans).

questionJson.question: Read this information report extract about the solar system. Use the checklist to evaluate it.
A pupil wants to add a conclusion to their information report about the water cycle. Which ending is BEST?

feedback.correct: ✅ Correct! Option C does three things a good conclusion must do: it restates the topic\'s significance ("most essential"), explains broader impact ("life as we know it"), and connects to a real-world issue ("climate change"). It leaves the reader with something to think about.
feedback.wrong: Option C is best. It restates the significance of the topic, explains why it matters beyond the report, and connects to a current real-world issue. A conclusion must do more than simply say "the end" – it should give the reader something lasting to consider.

questionJson.question: Progressive Writing Practice (PWP)
What is a diary or journal?

feedback.correct: Correct! A diary or journal is personal writing. It records the writer\'s own thoughts, feelings, and experiences.
feedback.wrong: A diary or journal is personal writing. It is not objective like an information report or news report. It records the writer\'s own private thoughts and experiences.

questionJson.question:
Read this opening:

"Monday, 4th September 2023

Dear Diary,

Today was my first day at Riverside Academy, and I need to write everything down before I forget how this day felt."

What two features appear in the very first two lines?

feedback.correct: Correct! Diary entries always begin with the date and a greeting. "Dear Diary" or "Dear Journal" is the standard opening greeting.
feedback.wrong: The two features are the date ("Monday, 4th September 2023") and the greeting ("Dear Diary"). These always appear at the start of a diary or journal entry.

questionJson.question: Which of these sentences is written in the correct style for a diary entry?

feedback.correct: Correct! Option C uses first person ("I"), past tense for the action ("sat"), and personal emotional detail ("tried not to look as lost as I felt"). These are the hallmarks of diary writing.
feedback.wrong: Option C is correct. It uses first person ("I"), past tense for the event, and personal emotional detail. The other sentences are from an information report (A), a news report (B), or a water cycle report (D).

questionJson.question: Read this diary extract. Then identify the TWO tenses being used and explain why.\n\n"I am still nervous about tomorrow, but tonight I feel grateful. Grateful for Maya\'s kindness, for Mr Chen\'s understanding, and for getting through this day without crying."

feedback.correct: Correct! Diary entries use BOTH tenses. Past tense for events that happened. Present tense for feelings that still exist now. This mix is what makes diary writing feel alive and immediate.
feedback.wrong: Diary entries use both tenses. Past tense for completed events. Present tense for feelings that are still happening. "I feel grateful" uses present because the feeling is happening now. "Getting through this day" uses past because the day is over.

questionJson.question:
Read this sentence from the talent show diary:

"Courage is not absence of fear. Courage is being terrified and doing it anyway."

What does this type of sentence do in a diary entry?

feedback.correct: Correct! This is a reflective conclusion. The writer draws a lesson or insight from their experience. Strong diary entries always move from events to feelings to reflection. The reflection is what makes a diary more than just a list of events.
feedback.wrong: This is a reflective thought – the writer draws a life lesson from their experience. Reflection is what separates a strong diary from a simple list of events. It shows the writer thinking about what their experience means.

questionJson.question: Model 2 (Discovering a Talent) and Model 1 (First Day at School) are both diaries, but they differ in one structural way. Which feature is present in Model 1 but NOT in Model 2?

feedback.correct: Correct! Model 1 opens with "Dear Diary" – the traditional greeting. Model 2 begins with the date but no greeting. Both are valid diary styles. Real diaries vary widely in format.
feedback.wrong: The answer is "Dear Diary". Model 1 has this traditional greeting; Model 2 begins with the date alone. Both are valid diary styles. Knowing this shows understanding of the genre\'s flexibility.

questionJson.question: Match each diary feature to its correct purpose.
questionJson.instruction: Match each item on the left to its pair on the right.

questionJson.question: These four sentences are from the camping trip diary. Which would come FIRST if they were put in the correct chronological order?

feedback.correct: Correct! The arrival at the campsite is the starting event. Diary entries are usually chronological – morning to evening, or beginning of event to end. Reflection ("I learned something") always comes last.
feedback.wrong: Option C is first – arriving at the campsite. Diary entries are chronological. Events happen in order: arrival first, tent trouble next, rain later, and reflection last.

questionJson.question:
Read these two versions of the same diary sentence:

Version A: "The canteen contained many pupils eating their lunch."
Version B: "Walking into that huge canteen alone, searching for somewhere to sit, made my heart pound."

Version B is better for a diary. Which THREE features make it better?

feedback.correct: Correct! Version B uses personal emotional detail ("heart pound"), descriptive language ("huge canteen alone"), and implicit first person (the experience is clearly the writer\'s). These are the three features that make diary writing vivid and personal.
feedback.wrong: Version B is better because of: personal emotional detail ("heart pound"), descriptive language ("huge canteen alone"), and first-person perspective. Diaries need all three to feel personal and immediate.

questionJson.question: Match each diary model to its most distinctive structural feature.
questionJson.instruction: Match each item on the left to its pair on the right.

questionJson.question: Complete this diary opening using the correct words. Both words are present tense because they describe feelings happening NOW.
questionJson.template: Complete this diary opening using the correct words. Both words are present tense because they describe feelings happening NOW.
questionJson.feedback: Correct! "Am" and "feel" are both present tense. In diary writing, present tense is used for feelings that exist right now, even though the entry is about past events.

questionJson.question:
A pupil has written this diary sentence:

"The workshop is very nice and Mrs Patel explains about painting."

How should they improve it for a diary entry?

feedback.correct: Correct! Option A uses sensory detail (smell of paint), specific visual detail (paint on dungarees), and personal reaction (most enthusiastic energy I have ever seen). This is exactly how strong diary writing works.
feedback.wrong: Option A is best. It uses sensory detail (smell of paint), visual specifics (paint on dungarees), and personal reaction (most enthusiastic energy I have ever seen). Vague language like "very nice" and "good" has no place in a diary.

questionJson.question:
Read this extract from Model 4:

"Here is the problem: I do love singing. In my bedroom. In the shower. While walking to school with headphones in. But the thought of singing in front of actual people makes my stomach clench with fear."

What writing technique is being used here?

feedback.correct: Correct! The fragmented sentences ("In my bedroom. In the shower.") mimic the natural flow of thought. This is stream of consciousness writing. It makes the reader feel they are inside the writer\'s mind.
feedback.wrong: This is stream of consciousness writing. The fragmented sentences ("In my bedroom. In the shower.") are not grammatically complete, but they mimic how thought actually works. This technique makes diary writing feel intimate and authentic.

questionJson.question: Complete this diary sentence. Choose a word that shows the writer\'s personal feeling about the event.
questionJson.template: Complete this diary sentence. Choose a word that shows the writer\'s personal feeling about the event.
questionJson.feedback: Correct! "Relieved" is a precise feeling word. Good diary writing uses specific emotional vocabulary rather than vague words like "good" or "happy". Other correct answers: "overjoyed", "grateful", "saved", "delighted".

questionJson.question: A pupil has written a diary entry but their teacher says it sounds like a list of events, not a diary. What is the main problem?\n\n"Today I went to school. I had maths. I had lunch. I walked home. I did my homework. I watched TV."

feedback.correct: Correct! A diary entry must include feelings, reactions, and reflection – not just a list of events. Without emotional content, it reads like a schedule. The test: could this have been written by anyone? If yes, it needs more personal feeling.
feedback.wrong: The problem is no feelings or reflection. A diary entry must tell the reader how the writer felt, not just what happened. The events are there, but the emotional response is missing. Without that, it is just a timetable.

questionJson.question: Write a diary entry opening (2-3 sentences) for this situation:\n\nSituation: You just found out you won a writing competition at school, but your best friend also entered and did not win.
questionJson.prompt: Write a diary entry opening (2-3 sentences) for this situation:\n\nSituation: You just found out you won a writing competition at school, but your best friend also entered and did not win.

questionJson.question:
Sophie's talent show diary uses a FLASHBACK structure. Read this opening:

"Today I did something that terrified me. I performed in front of 300 people. And I survived.

Let me back up and explain how I ended up in this terrifying situation..."

Why is the flashback structure effective for this diary entry?

feedback.correct: Correct! Telling the ending first ("I survived") then rewinding creates suspense. The reader already knows the outcome but wants to understand the journey. This is a sophisticated narrative technique that makes the diary much more engaging.
feedback.wrong: The flashback works by revealing the outcome first, then building the story leading to it. This creates suspense – we know she survived, but we want to know how she got there. It is a deliberate, sophisticated choice.

questionJson.question: Read this diary extract. Use the checklist to evaluate whether all key features are present.
A pupil wants to end their diary entry about a difficult day. Which ending is BEST?

feedback.correct: Correct! Option C is best because it: uses a present tense reflection (lying in bed, I keep thinking), includes a voice from a real person (what Aisha said), shows uncertainty honestly (I am not sure I believe her yet), and ends with hope (maybe tomorrow). This is reflective diary writing at its best.
feedback.wrong: Option C is best. It uses present tense for the ongoing reflection, quotes a real person, shows honest uncertainty, and ends with cautious hope. Strong diary conclusions do not summarise – they reflect, wonder, and look forward.

questionJson.question: Progressive Writing Practice (PWP)
What is an argument in writing?
options: A piece of writing that tells a story with characters and a plot.
A piece of writing that presents a position with reasons and evidence, either one-sided or balanced.
A piece of writing that gives instructions for completing a task.
A piece of writing that describes a topic using subheadings and technical vocabulary.

feedback.correct: Correct! Argument writing presents a position supported by reasons and evidence. It can be one-sided (for or against) or balanced (showing both sides).
feedback.wrong: Argument writing presents a position with reasons and evidence. It can be one-sided (arguing for one view) or balanced (showing both sides fairly).

questionJson.question:
Read this sentence:

"School uniforms benefit pupils, parents, and teachers in numerous important ways."

What is the purpose of this sentence in an argument?
options: It introduces a specific statistic to support a claim.
It is the position statement – it declares the writer\

feedback.correct: Correct! This is a position statement. It declares the writer\'s view (uniforms benefit everyone) at the very start of the argument. Every one-sided argument must open with a clear position statement.
feedback.wrong: This is a position statement – it declares the writer\'s view (uniforms benefit everyone) at the start. A position statement is the opening move in every one-sided argument.

questionJson.question: Which of these is a ONE-SIDED argument title?
options: Should Schools Require Uniforms? Considering All Perspectives.
School Uniforms: Arguments For and Against.
Why Every School Should Require Uniforms.
The School Uniform Debate: A Balanced View.

feedback.correct: Correct! "Why Every School Should Require Uniforms" is a one-sided title because it already declares a position (every school SHOULD). The other titles are balanced – they suggest both sides will be explored.
feedback.wrong: "Why Every School Should Require Uniforms" is one-sided because it declares a position in the title itself. Balanced argument titles use question format or language that signals both sides will be considered.

questionJson.question: Read these two passages. Which is the key difference between a ONE-SIDED argument and a BALANCED argument?
options: One-sided arguments are shorter; balanced arguments are longer.
One-sided arguments state a clear conclusion for one position; balanced arguments acknowledge both sides and reach a tentative or no conclusion.
One-sided arguments use subheadings; balanced arguments do not.
One-sided arguments use present tense; balanced arguments use past tense.

feedback.correct: Correct! This is the critical distinction. One-sided: clear position throughout, conclusion restates that position forcefully. Balanced: both sides given equal weight, conclusion is tentative ("perhaps", "may") or acknowledges complexity.
feedback.wrong: The key difference is the conclusion. One-sided = clear position stated and restated. Balanced = both sides respected, conclusion is tentative ("perhaps the solution lies in...") or avoids taking a strong position.

questionJson.question:
Read this extract from the homework argument:

"Professor Harris Cooper from Duke University concluded that homework shows zero correlation with academic achievement for primary pupils. Finland, consistently ranking among the world's top education systems, assigns minimal homework to primary children."

What TWO types of evidence are used here?
options: Personal opinion and emotional language.
Expert authority (Professor Cooper) and an international example (Finland).
Statistics and a direct quote from a student.
A rhetorical question and a counterargument.

feedback.correct: Correct! Expert authority (naming a specific professor from a named university) and an international example (Finland\'s education system) are two of the most powerful types of evidence in argument writing. Both make the claim harder to dismiss.
feedback.wrong: Two types of evidence: expert authority (Professor Cooper, Duke University) and an international example (Finland). Using a named expert and a real-world example strengthens an argument because readers find them harder to dismiss than a vague claim.

questionJson.question:
Read this sentence from the school uniforms argument:

"Some people argue that uniforms restrict personal expression and individuality. However, children have numerous other opportunities to express themselves through hairstyles, hobbies, artwork, writing, and friendships."

What technique is being used?
options: Stream of consciousness – the writer is exploring their own thoughts
Counterargument and rebuttal – the writer names the opposing view, then dismisses it
A balanced argument – the writer is presenting both sides equally
A flashback structure – the writer returns to an earlier point

feedback.correct: Correct! This is counterargument and rebuttal. The writer raises the opposing view ("some argue uniforms restrict expression"), then dismisses it ("however, children have numerous other ways to express themselves"). This is a key persuasive technique.
feedback.wrong: This is counterargument and rebuttal. The writer names the opposition ("some argue...") then dismisses it ("however..."). This technique strengthens a one-sided argument because it shows the writer has considered and rejected the opposing view.

questionJson.question: Match each argument writing feature to its correct definition.
questionJson.instruction: Match each item on the left to its pair on the right.

questionJson.question:
Read the list of logical connectives below. Sort them into the correct categories:

Firstly  |  However  |  Therefore  |  Furthermore  |  Nevertheless  |  Additionally  |  In conclusion  |  On the other hand

Which group is correct?
options: Adding arguments: Firstly, Furthermore, Additionally. Contrasting: However, Nevertheless, On the other hand. Concluding: Therefore, In conclusion.
Adding arguments: However, Nevertheless. Contrasting: Firstly, Furthermore. Concluding: Additionally.
Adding arguments: Therefore, In conclusion. Contrasting: Firstly, Additionally. Concluding: However.
Adding arguments: In conclusion, However, Therefore. Contrasting: Firstly, Furthermore. Concluding: Additionally.

feedback.correct: Correct! Adding arguments: Firstly, Furthermore, Additionally (these build the case). Contrasting: However, Nevertheless, On the other hand (these introduce opposition). Concluding: Therefore, In conclusion (these wrap up the argument).
feedback.wrong: Correct groupings: Adding = Firstly, Furthermore, Additionally. Contrasting = However, Nevertheless, On the other hand. Concluding = Therefore, In conclusion. Knowing which group to use is essential for structuring arguments logically.

questionJson.question: A pupil is writing a one-sided argument against screen time for children. They have four reasons. Which order creates the STRONGEST one-sided argument?
options: Most obvious reason first → least convincing reason → counterargument → conclusion
Strongest reason first → second strongest → counterargument addressed → conclusion restating position
Weakest reason first → gradually build to strongest → conclude with a question
Counterargument first → dismiss it → two reasons → position statement

feedback.correct: Correct! Strong argument structure: lead with your most persuasive reason, then build with further evidence, address and dismiss the counterargument near the end, then conclude by restating your position forcefully. This mirrors all four model texts.
feedback.wrong: Best structure: strongest reason first → build with further reasons → address and dismiss the counterargument → conclude by restating the position. This maximises persuasive impact.

questionJson.question: Match each extract to its argument type.
questionJson.instruction: Match each item on the left to its pair on the right.

questionJson.question: Complete this argument paragraph using the correct logical connective.
questionJson.template: Complete this argument paragraph using the correct logical connective.
questionJson.feedback: Correct! "Firstly" signals the first argument in a sequence. It is one of the most important logical connectives in argument writing. The pattern is: Firstly → Secondly → Furthermore → Additionally → In conclusion.

questionJson.question:
A pupil has written this argument sentence:

"School uniforms are nice and lots of people like them."

How should they improve it to meet the standard of the model texts?
options: "School uniforms are really nice and very lots of people like them a lot."
"Research from the University of Essex found that schools with uniforms reported 30% fewer instances of bullying related to appearance."
"I think school uniforms are quite good for some schools in some situations."
"School uniforms have been used for a long time and teachers think they are a good idea."

feedback.correct: Correct! Option B replaces vague opinion ("nice", "people like them") with a specific statistic from a named source (University of Essex, 30% figure). This is the gold standard for argument evidence.
feedback.wrong: Option B is correct. It replaces vague language ("nice", "lots of people like them") with a named source and a specific statistic. In argument writing, specific evidence always beats vague opinion.

questionJson.question:
Read this balanced argument conclusion:

"Both perspectives present reasonable arguments. Perhaps the solution lies in balanced approaches that maintain some consistency whilst allowing appropriate personal expression."

Which word signals this is a BALANCED argument conclusion?
options: "Both" – it signals that two sides are being acknowledged
The word "reasonable" – it shows the writer is being fair
The word "perhaps" – it signals uncertainty and avoids taking a firm position
The word "solution" – it signals the writer is concluding

feedback.correct: Correct! "Perhaps" is the key word. It signals tentativeness – the writer is NOT committing to a firm position. Balanced arguments use hedging language: perhaps, may, might, could, likely. One-sided arguments use definite language: clearly, certainly, must, should.
feedback.wrong: "Perhaps" is the key hedging word. Balanced arguments use tentative language (perhaps, may, might) to signal that the writer acknowledges complexity and does not commit to one side. One-sided arguments use definite language (clearly, must, certainly).

questionJson.question: Complete this one-sided argument rebuttal. The missing word should introduce the opposing view.
questionJson.template: Complete this one-sided argument rebuttal. The missing word should introduce the opposing view.
questionJson.feedback: Correct! "Some" introduces the counterargument softly – the writer acknowledges that others hold this view without endorsing it. Other valid openers: "Critics argue...", "Opponents claim...", "Those who disagree suggest..."

questionJson.question: A pupil has written this argument paragraph. What is the MAIN problem?\n\n"Homework is bad. Children do not like it. Parents do not like it. It takes a long time. It is boring. Nobody wants homework."
options: The paragraph is too long and needs to be shorter.
The paragraph makes claims but provides no evidence, statistics, or expert opinions to support them.
The paragraph is written in the wrong tense.
The paragraph is a balanced argument when it should be one-sided.

feedback.correct: Correct! The paragraph has opinions ("bad", "boring", "nobody wants") but no evidence. Compare: "Homework is bad" vs "Professor Harris Cooper from Duke University found homework shows zero correlation with academic achievement for primary pupils." Evidence transforms opinion into argument.
feedback.wrong: The problem is no evidence. Every claim ("bad", "takes a long time") is unsupported. Compare "homework is bad" with "Professor Cooper found zero correlation with achievement". Evidence is what separates argument writing from opinion writing.

questionJson.question: Write ONE argument paragraph for or against school uniforms. Include: a logical connective, a claim, and one piece of evidence.
questionJson.prompt: Write ONE argument paragraph for or against school uniforms. Include: a logical connective, a claim, and one piece of evidence.
questionJson.modelAnswer: Furthermore, uniforms eliminate the morning stress of choosing outfits. Many pupils waste valuable time each morning worrying about peer acceptance of their clothing choices. With uniforms, this decision disappears completely, streamlining morning routines and reducing family conflicts before school.

answerJson.modelAnswer: Furthermore, uniforms eliminate the morning stress of choosing outfits. Many pupils waste valuable time each morning worrying about peer acceptance of their clothing choices. With uniforms, this decision disappears completely, streamlining morning routines and reducing family conflicts before school.

questionJson.question: Which of these is the BEST balanced argument conclusion?
options: "In conclusion, reducing plastic use is completely essential and every school must ban plastic immediately."
"Ultimately, the best approach likely involves meaningful plastic reduction guided by careful planning, adequate resources, and inclusive decision-making involving teachers, parents, and pupils."
"In conclusion, plastic is bad and schools should do something about it. The end."
"Therefore, because both sides have been considered, the first side is actually more correct."

feedback.correct: Correct! Option B is the strongest balanced conclusion because it: acknowledges both sides implicitly ("meaningful" but "careful"), uses tentative language ("likely"), names specific realistic steps, and avoids taking an absolute position. It respects the complexity of the issue.
feedback.wrong: Option B is best. It uses tentative language ("likely"), acknowledges practical realities ("careful planning", "adequate resources"), and avoids declaring one side the winner. A balanced conclusion should leave the reader thinking, not telling them what to think.

questionJson.question: Read this extract from the one-sided school uniform argument. Use the checklist to evaluate it.
A pupil wants to write a balanced argument about banning mobile phones in schools. Which PLAN is correct for a balanced argument?
options: Title (question format) → Introduction (debate exists) → Arguments FOR phone ban → Arguments AGAINST phone ban → Finding middle ground / tentative conclusion
Title (declares position) → Position statement → Three reasons against phones → Dismiss counterargument → Strong conclusion restating position
Title → Statistics → One-sided conclusion → Counterargument → Greeting and sign-off
Introduction → Arguments for phones → Arguments for phones → Arguments for phones → Strong conclusion against phones

feedback.correct: Correct! A balanced argument plan: question-format title → introduction acknowledging the debate → one side\'s arguments → other side\'s arguments → finding middle ground or tentative conclusion. This mirrors both Models 2 and 4.
feedback.wrong: Option A is correct. A balanced argument: question title, introduction acknowledging complexity, both sides given equal treatment, tentative conclusion. Options B and D are one-sided structures.

questionJson.question: Progressive Writing Practice (PWP)
questionJson.modelAnswer: Primary schools should abolish homework since Professor Harris Cooper from Duke University concluded that it shows zero correlation with academic achievement for pupils aged 5-11.

questionJson.question: What is the key difference between a formal letter and an informal letter?
options: Formal letters are longer; informal letters are shorter.

Formal letters are written to people you do not know well or in official positions, using professional language; informal letters are written to people you know well, using friendly, conversational language.
Formal letters always use "Dear Sir or Madam"; informal letters never use "Dear".
Formal letters must be handwritten; informal letters can be typed.

feedback.correct: Correct! The key difference is audience and language. Formal = official relationship, professional language, no contractions. Informal = personal relationship, friendly language, contractions allowed.
feedback.wrong: The key difference is audience and language. Formal letters go to people in official positions (headteachers, editors) using professional language. Informal letters go to people you know well (friends, relatives) using friendly, conversational language.

questionJson.question:
Read this letter opening:

"Dear Mrs Bennett,

I am writing to request your permission to establish a recycling club at Riverside Primary School."

What TWO features identify this as a formal letter opening?
options: First name greeting and short sentence.
Title and surname in greeting ("Mrs Bennett") and formal purpose statement ("I am writing to request").
No address at the top and a question mark.
Use of contractions and exclamation marks.

feedback.correct: Correct! "Mrs Bennett" (title + surname) signals formal register. "I am writing to request" is the standard formal purpose statement. Both features appear in all formal model letters.
feedback.wrong: Two formal features: "Mrs Bennett" (title + surname – never first name in formal letters) and "I am writing to request" (the standard formal opening phrase that states purpose immediately).

questionJson.question: Which sign-off is used when you DO NOT know the name of the person you are writing to?
options: "Yours sincerely"
"Lots of love"
"Yours faithfully"
"Best wishes"

feedback.correct: Correct! "Yours faithfully" is used when you do not know the recipient\'s name (e.g. "Dear Sir or Madam"). "Yours sincerely" is used when you know their name (e.g. "Dear Mrs Bennett"). This rule is tested in formal writing assessments.
feedback.wrong: "Yours faithfully" = name unknown (Dear Sir or Madam). "Yours sincerely" = name known (Dear Mrs Bennett). This is a formal convention that must be applied correctly.

questionJson.question:
Read these two greetings:

A: "Dear Maya,"
B: "Dear Sir or Madam,"

Which is formal and which is informal? What does each greeting signal to the reader?
options: A is formal (uses "Dear"); B is informal (uses "Sir").
A is informal (first name only – signals personal relationship); B is formal (no name known – signals official/unknown recipient).
Both are formal because both use "Dear".
Both are informal because neither uses a full surname.

feedback.correct: Correct! First name only = informal, personal relationship. "Sir or Madam" = formal, name unknown. The greeting alone signals the entire register of the letter before the reader sees a single sentence.
feedback.wrong: A is informal (first name = personal relationship). B is formal (Sir or Madam = name unknown, official relationship). The greeting is the first register signal – it sets up everything that follows.

questionJson.question:
Read this sentence from the formal recycling club letter:

"I would be grateful if you would consider this proposal."

Why is this phrasing used instead of "Please say yes"?
options: It is longer and therefore more persuasive.
It uses conditional phrasing ("would... if") to show politeness and respect for the recipient\

feedback.correct: Correct! "I would be grateful if you would consider..." uses conditional phrasing to show deference to the headteacher\'s authority. Formal letters acknowledge the recipient\'s power to decide. This is very different from L55\'s confident argument language.
feedback.wrong: Conditional phrasing ("I would... if you would") signals politeness and respect for the recipient\'s authority. Formal letters to people in positions of power use this deferential tone. It is deliberately different from the confident language of one-sided arguments.

questionJson.question:
Read this extract from the informal holiday letter:

"You would love the beach here. It is tucked into a little cove with rock pools at both ends. Yesterday, my brother and I spent three hours exploring and found the most incredible creatures. Can you blame it?"

What THREE features mark this as informal writing?
options: Long sentences, formal vocabulary, and a polite sign-off.
Direct address to reader ("You would love"), personal anecdote (exploring rock pools), and a rhetorical question as humour ("Can you blame it?").
No sender\

feedback.correct: Correct! Direct address to the reader ("You would love") creates intimacy. Personal anecdotes make the letter feel conversational. Rhetorical questions used for humour ("Can you blame it?") are informal devices impossible in formal letters.
feedback.wrong: Three informal features: direct address ("You would love"), personal anecdote (exploring rock pools), and rhetorical question for humour ("Can you blame it?"). None of these belong in formal letters.

questionJson.question: Sort these letter phrases into the correct column.\n\n"Furthermore, I would like to draw your attention to..." | "I was absolutely thrilled when..." | "I am available to meet at your convenience" | "Can you believe we saw an actual starfish?!" | "On behalf of many grateful young people..." | "Miss you loads, hope you\'re well!"
options: Formal: "Furthermore...", "I am available...", "On behalf of..."\nInformal: "I was absolutely thrilled...", "Can you believe...", "Miss you loads..."
Formal: "I was absolutely thrilled...", "Can you believe...", "On behalf of..."\nInformal: "Furthermore...", "I am available...", "Miss you loads..."
All phrases are formal because they all use complete sentences.
All phrases are informal because they all use first person.

feedback.correct: Correct! Formal: "Furthermore...", "I am available at your convenience", "On behalf of..." (all use professional phrasing). Informal: "absolutely thrilled" (personal emotion), "Can you believe...?!" (exclamation question), "Miss you loads" (contraction and casual language).
feedback.wrong: Formal = "Furthermore...", "available at your convenience", "On behalf of...". Informal = "absolutely thrilled", "Can you believe...?!", "Miss you loads". The key signals are vocabulary level, contractions, exclamations, and personal emotional language.

questionJson.question: A pupil is writing a formal letter to their local council to request a new bus shelter near their school. Which THREE features must their letter include?
options: Sender\
s address and date, formal greeting with title and surname OR "Dear Sir or Madam", and "Yours sincerely/faithfully" sign-off.
First name greeting, "Lots of love" sign-off, and personal anecdotes.
No address, "Dear Friend", and multiple exclamation marks.

feedback.correct: Correct! A formal letter must have: sender\'s address and date (layout), correct formal greeting (name known → title + surname; name unknown → Dear Sir or Madam), and correct formal sign-off (Yours sincerely if name known; Yours faithfully if unknown). These three are non-negotiable.
feedback.wrong: Three required formal features: address and date, correct formal greeting, and correct sign-off (Yours sincerely = name known; Yours faithfully = name unknown). These must always be present in formal letters.
