questionJson.question: Complete this formal letter opening using the correct phrase.
questionJson.template: Complete this formal letter opening using the correct phrase.
questionJson.feedback: Correct! "I am writing to request" is the standard formal opening phrase. Other formal openers: "I am writing to inform you", "I am writing to enquire about", "I am writing to express my concern about". These always state the purpose immediately.

questionJson.question:
A pupil has written this sentence in a formal letter:

"I think it's really unfair that our playground doesn't have any new equipment and I want you to do something about it."

How should they rewrite it for a formal letter?
options: "I think it\
t have new equipment."

"I would like to respectfully draw your attention to the lack of updated play equipment at our school, which I believe presents both a safety concern and a missed opportunity for physical development."
"I am writing to say the playground equipment is not good and please fix it."
"IT IS VERY UNFAIR THAT WE DON\

feedback.correct: Correct! Option B removes contractions ("it\'s", "doesn\'t"), uses formal vocabulary ("respectfully draw your attention", "physical development"), frames the issue professionally, and avoids demand language. This is the formal register transformation.
feedback.wrong: Option B is correct. It removes contractions, replaces demand language ("do something about it") with respectful phrasing ("draw your attention"), and adds professional framing ("safety concern", "physical development"). These are the formal register requirements.

questionJson.question:
Read this paragraph from the informal holiday letter:

"I cannot believe we have been here five days already. Our cottage is absolutely adorable with wonky sloping floors and tiny doorways that Dad keeps banging his head on. Mum says it is over 300 years old. People must have been shorter back then!"

Which feature makes this clearly informal rather than formal?
options: The use of past tense throughout
The use of humorous personal observation ("People must have been shorter back then!") which would never appear in a formal letter
The mention of family members by relationship (Mum, Dad)
The use of a date at the top of the letter

feedback.correct: Correct! Humorous personal observation ("People must have been shorter back then!") is a hallmark of informal writing. It assumes the reader will find it funny, implies a shared sense of humour, and treats the letter as entertainment rather than communication. Formal letters never do this.
feedback.wrong: The humorous observation is the clearest informal signal. It assumes shared humour, entertains the reader, and treats the letter casually. Formal letters report and request; informal letters entertain, share, and connect.

questionJson.question: Complete this sign-off correctly for a formal letter where the writer KNOWS the recipient\'s name.
questionJson.template: Complete this sign-off correctly for a formal letter where the writer KNOWS the recipient\'s name.
questionJson.feedback: Correct! "Yours sincerely" is used when the recipient\'s name is known (you opened with "Dear Mrs Bennett"). "Yours faithfully" is used when the name is unknown (you opened with "Dear Sir or Madam"). The rule: sincerely = name known; faithfully = name unknown.

questionJson.question:
A pupil has written this formal letter paragraph:

"Also, everyone in my class thinks the library should have more books. We asked around and most people said yes. So you should get more books. The library is quite good though."

What are the TWO main problems?
options: The sentences are too short and the letter is too informal.
The language is informal ("asked around", "quite good though") and the evidence is vague ("most people said yes") rather than specific.
The paragraph has no connectives and uses past tense.
The paragraph is too long and needs to be shorter.

feedback.correct: Correct! Two problems: informal language ("asked around", "quite good though", "So you should") and vague evidence ("most people said yes" – how many? what percentage?). A strong formal letter paragraph uses precise vocabulary and specific evidence, exactly as in argument writing.
feedback.wrong: Two problems: informal register ("asked around", "quite good though") and vague evidence ("most people" is not precise enough). Formal letters use specific evidence: "A survey of 23 Year 5 pupils found that 87% would use the library more if additional fiction titles were available."

questionJson.question: Write ONE paragraph for a formal letter to your headteacher requesting a school garden. Include: purpose statement, one specific piece of evidence or reasoning, and a polite request.
questionJson.prompt: Write ONE paragraph for a formal letter to your headteacher requesting a school garden. Include: purpose statement, one specific piece of evidence or reasoning, and a polite request.
questionJson.modelAnswer: I am writing to request your permission to establish a school garden at Riverside Primary. Research conducted by the Royal Horticultural Society demonstrates that school gardens improve pupils\' understanding of healthy eating and science concepts whilst providing valuable outdoor learning opportunities. I would be most grateful if you would consider allocating a small area of the school grounds for this purpose.

answerJson.modelAnswer: I am writing to request your permission to establish a school garden at Riverside Primary. Research conducted by the Royal Horticultural Society demonstrates that school gardens improve pupils\' understanding of healthy eating and science concepts whilst providing valuable outdoor learning opportunities. I would be most grateful if you would consider allocating a small area of the school grounds for this purpose.

questionJson.question: Read these two versions of the same letter closing. Which is correct for a formal letter?
options: "Anyway, I hope you take this seriously. Looking forward to hearing back. Cheers!"\n\n"Tom"

"I look forward to hearing from you at your earliest convenience.

Yours sincerely,

Tom Williams
Tom Williams (Year 5)"
"I think you should reply quickly. Let me know what you decide.\n\nTom, Year 5"
"That is everything I wanted to say. Thanks for reading.\n\nLots of love, Tom xxx"

feedback.correct: Correct! Option B uses: "I look forward to hearing from you" (formal future-looking close), "Yours sincerely" (correct formal sign-off with name known), printed name and year group (formal identification). All other options use informal language or incorrect sign-offs.
feedback.wrong: Option B is correct. "I look forward to hearing from you" is the standard formal closing phrase. "Yours sincerely" followed by signature space and printed name is the correct format. Year group adds identification for school letters.

questionJson.question: Read this extract from the formal recycling club letter. Use the checklist to evaluate it.

A pupil must choose the correct approach for this task:

"Write a letter to your pen pal in Australia describing your school trip to a museum."

Which approach is correct?
options: Write a formal letter because it involves a school trip and school topics should be formal.
Write an informal letter because a pen pal is a personal relationship; use a first name greeting, contractions, personal anecdotes, friendly language, and an informal sign-off.
Write a formal letter because the topic (a museum) is educational and therefore serious.
Write an informal letter but use "Yours faithfully" because the pen pal\

feedback.correct: Correct! A pen pal is a personal relationship, so informal register is correct. Use first name in greeting, contractions, personal anecdotes about the trip, questions to the friend, and informal sign-off (Lots of love / Best wishes). The topic (school trip) does not make the letter formal – the relationship does.
feedback.wrong: Option B is correct. The register is determined by the RELATIONSHIP, not the topic. Pen pal = personal relationship = informal. Use first name greeting, contractions, friendly language, personal tone, and informal sign-off (Lots of love/Best wishes). "Yours faithfully" would never appear in an informal letter.

answerJson.modelAnswer: Formal: Dear Dr Patel, I am writing to enquire about the possibility of our class visiting the Natural History Museum as part of our current science topic on ecosystems.\nInformal: Dear Grandma, Thank you so much for the wonderful book token – I have already chosen three novels I have been desperate to read.

questionJson.question: What is the key difference between an explanation and a set of instructions?
options: Explanations are always longer than instructions.
An explanation answers HOW or WHY something works; a set of instructions tells the reader WHAT TO DO in order to complete a task.
Explanations use subheadings; instructions never do.
Instructions are always written about cooking; explanations are always about science.

feedback.correct: Correct! Explanations answer HOW or WHY (How does a bicycle work? Why do leaves change colour?). Instructions tell the reader WHAT TO DO, step by step (How to make a paper aeroplane). Both genres are non-fiction, but their purpose is completely different.
feedback.wrong: The key difference is purpose. Explanation = HOW or WHY something works (reader learns and understands). Instructions = WHAT TO DO (reader follows and produces). This distinction drives every other language choice.

questionJson.question:
Read this sentence:

"When a rider pushes pedals with their feet, the pedals turn the cranks, which rotate the front chainring."

Is this from an explanation or a set of instructions? What is the key signal?
options: Instructions – because it describes something physical happening.
Explanation – because it describes a process using causal language ("when") and third person ("a rider"), with no imperative verb.
Instructions – because it mentions specific parts of a bicycle.
Explanation – because it uses technical vocabulary.

feedback.correct: Correct! Three signals: (1) causal language "when" linking cause and effect, (2) third person "a rider" – not telling YOU to do something, (3) no imperative verb. Explanations describe processes; instructions command actions.
feedback.wrong: This is an explanation. The signals: "when" (causal language), "a rider" (third person – not "you"), and no imperative verb. Instructions would say "Push the pedals" (imperative, second person). Explanations say "when the rider pushes" (descriptive, third person).

questionJson.question: Which of these is an imperative verb as used in instructions?
options: Chlorophyll absorbs red and blue light.
The chainring rotates when the rider pushes the pedals.
Fold the paper in half lengthways and crease firmly.
Spinning wheels create gyroscopic stability.

feedback.correct: Correct! "Fold" is an imperative verb – it gives a direct command to the reader. Imperative verbs are the defining language feature of instructions. Other imperative verbs in the model texts: Place, Blend, Pour, Peel, Add, Press, Repeat.
feedback.wrong: "Fold" is the imperative verb. It commands the reader directly. The other sentences use third person descriptive verbs (absorbs, rotates, create) which belong to explanations, not instructions.

questionJson.question:
Read these two texts:

A: "The drivetrain transfers power from the rider's legs to the rear wheel, making the bicycle move forward."

B: "Place banana chunks into the blender first. Add frozen berries on top. Pour in yogurt and milk."

What are the THREE key differences?
options: A uses past tense; B uses present tense; A has subheadings.
A uses third person and causal description; B uses imperative verbs and sequential numbered steps; A explains a process while B tells the reader what to do.
A is formal; B is informal; A has more technical vocabulary.
A is an introduction; B is a conclusion; both use present tense.

feedback.correct: Correct! Three key differences: (1) Person – A uses third person ("the drivetrain transfers"), B implies second person ("Place" = you place). (2) Verb type – A uses descriptive verbs, B uses imperative verbs. (3) Purpose – A explains why/how, B commands what to do.
feedback.wrong: Three differences: person (A = third person description, B = imperative commands), verb type (A = descriptive, B = imperative), purpose (A = explain, B = instruct). These three differences apply to every explanation vs instruction distinction.

questionJson.question:
Read this extract from the autumn leaves explanation:

"As autumn approaches, two environmental changes trigger transformation. First, days become shorter, meaning leaves receive fewer sunlight hours. Second, temperatures drop, especially at night. These changes signal to trees that winter is approaching."

What type of language is being used throughout this passage?
options: Imperative language – commanding the reader to do something.
Causal language – explaining cause-and-effect relationships between events and conditions.
Persuasive language – arguing that autumn is the best season.
Informal language – chatting to the reader like a friend.

feedback.correct: Correct! Causal language explains why things happen. Key causal words and phrases here: "trigger" (cause), "meaning" (result), "signal to" (cause-and-effect), "approaching" (leading to). Causal language is the defining feature of explanations – it shows the chain of cause and effect.
feedback.wrong: This is causal language – it explains why things happen. Causal signals: "trigger", "meaning", "signal to". Causal language chains are the heart of every explanation. Instructions never use causal language; they use imperative commands.

questionJson.question: A set of instructions must include certain features. Which combination is correct?
options: A title, subheadings, causal language, present tense descriptions, third person throughout.
A title stating the task, a materials or ingredients list, numbered steps with imperative verbs, precise measurements or details, tips or troubleshooting.
A position statement, evidence, counterargument, and a tentative conclusion.
A date, greeting, purpose statement, and Yours sincerely.

feedback.correct: Correct! Instructions always include: title (states the task), materials/ingredients list (what is needed), numbered steps (sequence), imperative verbs (commands), precise measurements or details (accuracy), and often tips or troubleshooting. These features appear in both Model 3 (paper aeroplane) and Model 4 (smoothie recipe).
feedback.wrong: Instructions require: title (stating the task), materials/ingredients, numbered steps, imperative verbs, and precise details. Options A, C, and D describe other genres (explanation, argument, letter).

questionJson.question: Match each text extract to its genre and key feature.
questionJson.instruction: Match each item on the left to its pair on the right.

questionJson.question: Sort these words and phrases into the correct category.\n\n"because" | "Blend" | "when" | "as a result" | "Fold" | "Pour" | "this causes" | "Place"
options: Causal language (explanation): "because", "when", "as a result", "this causes"\nImperative verbs (instructions): "Blend", "Fold", "Pour", "Place"
Causal language: "Blend", "when", "Pour"\nImperative verbs: "because", "Fold", "this causes"
Causal language: "because", "Blend", "as a result"\nImperative verbs: "when", "Fold", "Pour"
All words can be used in both explanations and instructions.

feedback.correct: Correct! Causal language (explanation): because, when, as a result, this causes – all link cause to effect. Imperative verbs (instructions): Blend, Fold, Pour, Place – all command the reader to act. Keeping these two categories separate is the key to writing the correct genre.
feedback.wrong: Correct groupings: Causal language = because, when, as a result, this causes. Imperative verbs = Blend, Fold, Pour, Place. Causal language explains WHY; imperative verbs tell you WHAT TO DO.

questionJson.question: A pupil is writing instructions for how to plant a seed. Which of these step openings is CORRECT for instructions?
options: The seed is placed into the soil and covered with approximately one centimetre of compost.
Place the seed into the soil and cover it with approximately one centimetre of compost.
Planting the seed into the soil with compost is the next stage in the process.
Seeds need to be placed in soil. Compost is also important.

feedback.correct: Correct! Option B uses an imperative verb ("Place") followed by specific detail ("approximately one centimetre"). This is the correct instruction step format: imperative verb + object + precise detail. Option A uses passive voice (the seed is placed) and Option D lacks precision.
feedback.wrong: Option B is correct: "Place" (imperative verb) + "the seed into the soil" (specific action) + "approximately one centimetre" (precise measurement). Instructions = imperative + specific detail. Never use passive voice or vague language.

questionJson.instruction: Match each item on the left to its pair on the right.

questionJson.question: A pupil writes: "When a person pushes the pedals, the pedals turned the cranks."\n\nWhat is the problem, and how should it be fixed?
options: The sentence is too short. Add more detail.
Tense inconsistency: "pushes" (present) and "turned" (past) clash. Fix: "When a rider pushes the pedals, the pedals turn the cranks." – consistent present tense throughout.
The sentence uses "when" which is not allowed in explanations.
The sentence needs an imperative verb to begin.

feedback.correct: Correct! "Pushes" is present tense but "turned" is past tense. Explanations use present tense throughout because the process is always happening. Fix: "When a rider pushes the pedals, the pedals turn the cranks." Both verbs in present tense.
feedback.wrong: Tense inconsistency: "pushes" (present) clashes with "turned" (past). Explanations use present tense throughout – the process is always happening, not something that happened once. Fix: "...the pedals turn the cranks."

questionJson.question: Complete this explanation sentence with the correct causal word.
questionJson.template: Complete this explanation sentence with the correct causal word.
questionJson.feedback: Correct! "Because" is the most direct causal connective – it links the observation (green leaves) to its cause (chlorophyll). Other causal connectives: when, as a result, this causes, therefore, consequently.

questionJson.question:
Read this instruction step:

"Blend the smoothie for a while until it looks smooth."

How should this be improved to meet the standard of the model texts?
options: "Blend the smoothie for quite a long time, checking it carefully."
"Blend for 30 to 40 seconds at high speed until completely smooth with no visible chunks."
"You should blend the smoothie until it seems smooth enough."
"Blending the smoothie until smooth is the next step to complete."

feedback.correct: Correct! Option B adds precise timing (30 to 40 seconds), specific speed (high speed), and a clear endpoint (no visible chunks). Vague language ("a while", "looks smooth") fails instructions because the reader cannot follow imprecise guidance. Precise detail is essential.
feedback.wrong: Option B is correct. It replaces vague language ("a while", "looks smooth") with precise detail (30–40 seconds, high speed, no visible chunks). Instructions must be precise enough for a complete stranger to follow successfully.

questionJson.question: Complete this instruction step with the correct imperative verb.
questionJson.template: Complete this instruction step with the correct imperative verb.
questionJson.feedback: Correct! "Peel" is an imperative verb commanding the reader to act. Every step in a recipe or set of instructions opens with an imperative verb. Other recipe imperatives from Model 4: Gather, Place, Add, Pour, Blend, Check.

questionJson.question:
A pupil has written this explanation paragraph:

"Bicycles are good. They have wheels and pedals. You push the pedals and the bike moves. This is because of the chain. The chain is important. It connects things together."

What are the TWO main problems?
options: The paragraph is too long and uses past tense.

The language is vague and imprecise (no technical vocabulary), and the causal explanation is incomplete – it says "because of the chain" but never explains the mechanism (chainring, sprocket, how the chain transfers power).
The paragraph uses imperative verbs incorrectly.
The paragraph lacks a personal opinion and a sign-off.

feedback.correct: Correct! Two problems: (1) No technical vocabulary – "the chain connects things together" compared to "the chain loops around the chainring and connects to a sprocket attached to the rear wheel". (2) Incomplete causal chain – "because of the chain" is not an explanation. Explanations must show the full mechanism, step by step.
feedback.wrong: Two problems: vague language (no technical vocabulary like chainring, sprocket) and incomplete causal chain ("because of the chain" explains nothing). Strong explanations name specific parts and trace the full cause-and-effect mechanism.

questionJson.question: Write ONE explanation sentence about how something works in nature or science. Use causal language and at least one technical term.
questionJson.prompt: Write ONE explanation sentence about how something works in nature or science. Use causal language and at least one technical term.
questionJson.modelAnswer: When sunlight passes through water droplets in the atmosphere, each droplet acts as a tiny prism, refracting and separating the white light into its component wavelengths. Because different wavelengths bend at different angles, the colours appear separated, creating the arc of a rainbow.

answerJson.modelAnswer: When sunlight passes through water droplets in the atmosphere, each droplet acts as a tiny prism, refracting and separating the white light into its component wavelengths. Because different wavelengths bend at different angles, the colours appear separated, creating the arc of a rainbow.

questionJson.question: Which of these is the BEST opening for a set of instructions about making a bird feeder?
options: "I am going to tell you how to make a bird feeder. It is fun and easy to do."

"HOW TO MAKE A BIRD FEEDER
A bird feeder is a simple garden project that attracts birds whilst recycling household materials. Follow these steps carefully to create a feeder that will attract birds to your garden.

Materials Needed
- One empty plastic bottle
- String (approximately 50cm)
- Scissors
- Bird seed"
"Making a bird feeder is something you might want to do sometime if you like birds and have some materials available."
"Bird feeders are very popular. Many people have them. They help birds. You might like one."

feedback.correct: Correct! Option B has all required features: clear title (HOW TO MAKE...), brief explanation of what will be made and its purpose, instructions to follow carefully, and a complete materials list with precise quantities. This is the standard opening for any set of instructions.
feedback.wrong: Option B is correct. It includes: title with task stated, brief introduction explaining what and why, a follow instruction, and a precise materials list. Vague, informal, or first-person openings (Options A, C, D) do not meet the instructions standard.

questionJson.question: Read this extract from Model 1 (How a Bicycle Works). Use the checklist to evaluate it.

A teacher asks a pupil to write instructions for how to wash their hands. The pupil writes:

"Washing hands is important because bacteria cause illness. You should wash them before eating and after using the toilet. Water and soap are both needed for this."

What is the fundamental problem?
options: The instructions are too short.

This is an explanation, not a set of instructions. It explains WHY to wash hands (bacteria cause illness) using causal language and third person, instead of telling the reader HOW by using imperative verbs and numbered steps.
The vocabulary is too simple.
The instructions need a title and a materials list.

feedback.correct: Correct! The pupil has written an explanation (why handwashing matters) instead of instructions (how to wash your hands). Instructions would say: "Turn on the tap. Wet your hands thoroughly. Apply soap and rub your hands together for 20 seconds." Imperative verbs, numbered steps, precise detail. No causal language.
feedback.wrong: This is an explanation, not instructions. It answers WHY (bacteria cause illness) instead of WHAT TO DO. Instructions would use imperative verbs (Turn, Wet, Apply, Rub) and numbered steps. Never causal language ("because", "cause") in the body of instructions.

questionJson.question: What is a biography?
options: A fictional story about an imaginary character\
s life written in third person, usually in chronological order, highlighting their key achievements and lasting impact.
An explanation of how a famous person\

feedback.correct: Correct! A biography is a factual account of a real person\'s life, written by someone else. Key features: third person (she, he, they), chronological order, specific dates and facts, key achievements, and a legacy section showing lasting impact.
feedback.wrong: A biography is a factual account of a real person\'s life written by someone else (not an autobiography). Key features: third person, chronological order, specific facts and dates, achievements highlighted, legacy section.

questionJson.question:
Read this biography title:

"FLORENCE NIGHTINGALE (1820-1910): THE LADY WITH THE LAMP"

What THREE pieces of information does this title give the reader?
options: The person\
s full name, the dates they lived (birth and death years), and a descriptive phrase capturing their achievement or character.
The person\
s name, who their parents were, and where they died.

feedback.correct: Correct! Biography titles follow a standard format: FULL NAME (birth year–death year): DESCRIPTIVE PHRASE. The descriptive phrase captures the person\'s most famous achievement or quality. "The Lady with the Lamp" immediately signals compassion, night rounds, and nursing care.
feedback.wrong: Biography titles: FULL NAME (birth–death year): DESCRIPTIVE PHRASE. Name + life dates + achievement/character summary. All four model text titles follow this exact format.

questionJson.question: Which sentence correctly opens a biography?
options: I was born on 12th May 1820 in Florence, Italy, and I always wanted to be a nurse.
Florence Nightingale was a pioneering nurse who revolutionised healthcare and saved countless lives through her dedication to improving hospital conditions.
Once upon a time, there lived a kind woman named Florence who loved to help sick people.
Dear Diary, today I arrived at the hospital in Scutari and the conditions were absolutely horrifying.

feedback.correct: Correct! Option B is the correct biography opening. It uses: third person ("Florence Nightingale was"), past tense ("was"), a clear statement of who the person was, and immediately establishes their significance ("revolutionised healthcare"). It does not begin with birth details – that comes in the Early Life section.
feedback.wrong: Option B is correct. Biography openings: third person (never "I"), past tense, states who the person was, immediately establishes why they matter. The opening is not the same as the first life event – it is an overview of significance.

questionJson.question:
Read this extract from the Florence Nightingale biography:

"Florence often walked through the dark hospital wards at night carrying a lamp to check on patients. Soldiers called her 'The Lady with the Lamp' and drew comfort from her compassionate care. Her reforms dramatically reduced death rates from 42% to just 2%."

What technique makes this extract particularly effective as biography writing?
options: It uses first person to create a personal connection with the reader.
It combines a specific vivid detail (the lamp, the dark wards) with a powerful statistic (42% to 2%), showing character through action rather than stating Florence was kind.
It uses imperative verbs to tell the reader how to appreciate Florence\

feedback.correct: Correct! Two techniques working together: the specific visual detail (lamp, dark wards at night) shows Florence\'s dedication without the writer saying "Florence was dedicated". Then the statistic (42% to 2%) proves the impact. Biography shows character through chosen facts, not personal impressions.
feedback.wrong: Two techniques: specific visual detail (lamp, dark wards) shows character through action, and the statistic (42% to 2%) proves impact. Strong biography never says "she was kind" – it shows a kind action and lets the reader draw the conclusion.

questionJson.question:
Read these two sentences from the Alexander Graham Bell biography:

A: "On 10th March 1876, Alexander achieved his breakthrough. He spoke the famous words 'Mr Watson, come here, I want to see you' into his experimental device."

B: "Alexander invented the telephone in March 1876. It was very important."

Why is version A stronger?
options: Version A is shorter and therefore easier to read.
Version A gives the precise date (10th March), a specific quoted moment (the actual words spoken), and shows the achievement through a vivid scene. Version B is vague ("very important") and generic.
Version A uses formal vocabulary; Version B uses informal vocabulary.
Version A mentions Thomas Watson; Version B does not.

feedback.correct: Correct! Three elements make A stronger: precise date (10th March, not just "March"), specific quoted words (the actual first words ever spoken by telephone), and showing through a vivid scene. "Very important" is the weakest biography phrase – it states significance without proving it.
feedback.wrong: Version A is stronger because: precise date (10th March), specific quoted moment (actual words spoken), vivid scene (the reader is present for the moment). "Very important" is vague – biography must prove significance through specific facts, not claim it.

questionJson.question:
Look at these two sentences about Mary Anning from the biography:

A (body paragraph): "Mary Anning died of breast cancer on 9th March 1847, aged just 47."

B (legacy): "Today, Mary is celebrated as one of the greatest fossil hunters in history."

Why does sentence A use past tense and sentence B use present tense?
options: Sentence A is from an earlier chapter so uses past tense; sentence B is newer so uses present tense.
Sentence A describes a completed historical event (her death); sentence B describes her ongoing legacy and reputation, which continues to exist today.
Sentence A is more formal; sentence B is less formal.
Both should use past tense because the biography is about someone who has died.

feedback.correct: Correct! Two tenses are used deliberately in biography: past tense for completed historical events ("died", "discovered", "founded"), and present tense for the ongoing legacy ("is celebrated", "still read", "remains"). Her death happened once in 1847. Her reputation as a great fossil hunter continues today.
feedback.wrong: Past tense = completed events (death, birth, discovery). Present tense = ongoing legacy (reputation, influence, impact that continues today). This tense switch in the legacy section is a key biography convention.

questionJson.question: Match each biography section to its correct purpose.
questionJson.instruction: Match each item on the left to its pair on the right.

questionJson.question: Which of these sentences shows character through facts (the biography standard) rather than directly stating a quality?
options: "Florence Nightingale was very kind and dedicated."
"Florence often walked through the dark hospital wards at night carrying a lamp to check on every patient before she slept."
"Florence was a great person who helped many people."
"Florence Nightingale cared about her patients deeply."

feedback.correct: Correct! Option B shows dedication through a specific action (walking through dark wards at night, checking every patient). The reader draws the conclusion "she was kind and dedicated" from the evidence. Options A, C, and D state qualities directly without evidence – this is the weakest approach to biography writing.
feedback.wrong: Option B is correct. Biography principle: show character through specific chosen facts, never by stating qualities directly. "Walking through dark wards at night to check every patient" proves dedication. "She was very kind" claims dedication without proof.

questionJson.question: A biography must cover these four sections in order. Which sequence is correct?
options: Legacy → Key Achievement → Early Life → Introduction
Introduction → Early Life → Key Achievement(s) → Legacy
Key Achievement → Early Life → Introduction → Legacy
Legacy → Introduction → Key Achievement → Early Life

feedback.correct: Correct! Biography structure: Introduction (significance statement) → Early Life (context) → Key Achievement(s) (main body) → Legacy (ongoing impact). All four model texts follow this structure. The introduction is NOT the same as the earliest life event – it is the overview of significance.
feedback.wrong: Correct order: Introduction (who they were + why they matter) → Early Life (context/background) → Key Achievement(s) (main events) → Legacy (ongoing impact, present tense). This is the structure in all four model texts.

questionJson.question: Match each biography extract to the person it describes.
questionJson.instruction: Match each item on the left to its pair on the right.

questionJson.question: A pupil writes: "Florence Nightingale was a nurse. She helped soldiers. She was good at her job. She died in 1910."\n\nWhat are the TWO main weaknesses?
options: The sentences are too long and the vocabulary is too formal.
There is no significance statement (no sense of WHY Florence matters), and no specific facts, dates, or statistics to prove her impact ("good at her job" is vague).
The biography uses past tense when it should use present tense.
The biography is too short to read.

feedback.correct: Correct! Two problems: (1) No significance statement – "a nurse who helped soldiers" does not tell us why Florence matters. Compare: "a pioneering nurse who revolutionised healthcare". (2) Vague language – "good at her job" proves nothing. Compare: "reduced death rates from 42% to just 2%". Biography requires specific facts that prove significance.
feedback.wrong: Two problems: no significance (why does Florence matter beyond being a nurse?) and vague language ("good at her job" compared to "reduced death rates from 42% to 2%"). Both are solved by specific facts.

questionJson.question: Complete this biography opening sentence. Choose the word that establishes the person\'s significance immediately.
questionJson.template: Complete this biography opening sentence. Choose the word that establishes the person\'s significance immediately.
questionJson.feedback: Correct! "Changed" establishes significance immediately – it tells the reader that Mary Anning\'s work had a major impact, not just that she collected fossils. Alternatives: transformed, revolutionised, advanced. All opening sentences in the model texts immediately state the person\'s significance using a strong verb.

questionJson.question:
Read this legacy section:

"Florence Nightingale died on 13th August 1910, aged 90. Today, nurses worldwide honour her memory every year on International Nurses Day, celebrated on her birthday. Her lamp remains a powerful symbol of compassionate, professional healthcare."

What makes the final sentence particularly effective?
options: It is the longest sentence in the paragraph.
It uses present tense ("remains") and a specific symbolic image (the lamp) to connect Florence\

feedback.correct: Correct! "Her lamp remains a powerful symbol" uses present tense (ongoing legacy), a specific image (the lamp from the title), and a universal idea (compassionate, professional healthcare). This sentence connects everything: the title image, the personal story, and the broader significance. Legacy sections should always reach for wider meaning.
feedback.wrong: "Remains" (present tense) + specific symbolic image (the lamp, which appeared in the title) + universal significance (symbol of compassionate healthcare). Legacy sections connect the individual life to wider meaning. The best final sentences in biography circle back to the opening image.

questionJson.question: Complete this challenge section from a biography. The word should show what the person overcame.
questionJson.template: Complete this challenge section from a biography. The word should show what the person overcame.
questionJson.feedback: Correct! "Despite" signals that the person overcame obstacles. It creates the contrast between the challenges they faced and the achievements they produced. This contrast is one of the most powerful structures in biography writing.

questionJson.question:
A pupil is writing a biography of Beatrix Potter. They have written this body paragraph:

"Beatrix liked animals. She had rabbits and mice and other pets. She also drew a lot. She eventually wrote a book about a rabbit called Peter. People liked it. She wrote more books after that."

What THREE improvements would most strengthen this paragraph?
options: Add an introduction, make the sentences longer, and include more animals.

Add specific dates and ages ("At age 27", "in 1902"), replace vague claims with facts ("People liked it" → "became an immediate success"), and add the significance of what she did ("wrote 22 more beloved books").
Remove all the details about animals since they are not important.
Change all sentences to questions to make the biography more interesting.

feedback.correct: Correct! Three improvements: (1) specific dates and ages ("At age 27", "published in 1902"), (2) replace vague language ("people liked it" → "became an immediate success, adored by children everywhere"), and (3) add specific facts to prove significance ("22 more beloved books" rather than "more books after that"). These three changes transform a vague account into a strong biography paragraph.
feedback.wrong: Three improvements needed: specific dates/ages, specific facts replacing vague claims ("people liked it" → "became an immediate success"), and significance details ("22 more beloved books"). These are the differences between the pupil\'s draft and the Model 3 text.

questionJson.question: Write ONE biography paragraph about a real person you know or have studied. Include: a significance statement, a specific fact or date, and a detail that shows character through action.
questionJson.prompt: Write ONE biography paragraph about a real person you know or have studied. Include: a significance statement, a specific fact or date, and a detail that shows character through action.
questionJson.modelAnswer: Isambard Kingdom Brunel was an engineering genius who transformed nineteenth-century Britain through his bold, pioneering designs. In 1843, at the age of 37, he launched the SS Great Britain, the world\'s first large ocean-going ship to be driven by a screw propeller. Brunel did not design cautiously – he consistently chose the most ambitious solution to every engineering challenge, often working through the night on drawings when others had given up.

answerJson.modelAnswer: Isambard Kingdom Brunel was an engineering genius who transformed nineteenth-century Britain through his bold, pioneering designs. In 1843, at the age of 37, he launched the SS Great Britain, the world\'s first large ocean-going ship to be driven by a screw propeller. Brunel did not design cautiously – he consistently chose the most ambitious solution to every engineering challenge, often working through the night on drawings when others had given up.

questionJson.question: Which of these is the BEST legacy section to end a biography of Alexander Graham Bell?
options: "Alexander Graham Bell was a very important inventor. He did lots of useful things and people still remember him today. The telephone was very important for communication."

"Alexander Graham Bell died on 2nd August 1922 in Canada, aged 75. On the day of his funeral, telephone services across North America stopped for one minute in his honour. Today, billions of people worldwide use descendants of his invention every single day, making him one of history\

feedback.correct: Correct! Option B has: the death date with age (specific), the funeral tribute (shows his significance through a specific symbolic action), "Today" transition to legacy, the global scale ("billions of people"), and a final significance statement. Options A, C, and D are vague, personal, or informal.
feedback.wrong: Option B is correct. It uses: precise death date and age, a specific symbolic tribute (phone services stopped for one minute), "Today" transition to present-tense legacy, global scale ("billions"), and a significance conclusion. "Very important" and "quite a lot" are too vague for biography.

questionJson.question: Read this extract from the Mary Anning biography. Use the checklist to evaluate it.
A pupil is writing a biography of Beatrix Potter. They have finished writing about her children\'s books and now need to write the legacy section. Which approach is correct?
options: Summarise everything that was already written in the biography to remind the reader.
Open with her death date and age, then switch to present tense to show ongoing impact: "Today, millions of children worldwide still read Beatrix Potter\

feedback.correct: Correct! Legacy section structure: death date/age → present tense transition ("Today") → ongoing impact in the world → significance statement connecting to wider meaning. The tense switch from past to present marks the transition from historical events to living legacy. Personal opinions and summaries do not belong here.
feedback.wrong: Option B is correct. Legacy section: death date + age → "Today" transition → present tense for ongoing impact ("still read", "remain") → significance statement. The tense shift to present tense signals ongoing impact.

questionJson.question: Progressive Writing Practice (PWP)
questionJson.modelAnswer: Alexander Graham Bell filed his patent for the telephone on 7th March 1876, just hours before a rival inventor submitted a similar application, securing his place in history as the device\'s official inventor.

answerJson.modelAnswer: Alexander Graham Bell filed his patent for the telephone on 7th March 1876, just hours before a rival inventor submitted a similar application, securing his place in history as the device\'s official inventor.

questionJson.question: What is the main purpose of a poster or advertisement?
options: To explain in detail how something works using causal language and technical vocabulary.
To persuade the reader to take a specific action – attend an event, help find a pet, change a behaviour, or join a challenge.
To tell the factual story of a real person\

feedback.correct: Correct! Posters and advertisements are persuasive writing – they want the reader to DO something. Every choice (headline, image, colour, facts, tone) is made with that action in mind.
feedback.wrong: Posters and advertisements are persuasive writing. Their purpose is to make the reader take a specific action: attend, help, change behaviour, join. Every element exists to serve that purpose.

questionJson.question:
Look at these two headlines:

A: "Information about the Summer Fete"
B: "FUN FOR ALL THE FAMILY! ★ RIVERSIDE PRIMARY SCHOOL SUMMER FETE ★"

Why is Headline B more effective as a poster headline?
options: Headline B is longer and therefore contains more information.
Headline B uses capitals, symbols (★), and an exclamation mark to create excitement and visual impact. It sells the experience ("FUN FOR ALL THE FAMILY") before stating the event name.
Headline A is better because it is clear and factual.
Headline B uses too many words and should be shorter.

feedback.correct: Correct! Headline B works because: capitals create visual weight, ★ symbols add visual interest, "FUN FOR ALL THE FAMILY" sells the experience before naming the event, and the exclamation mark signals enthusiasm. Headline A is a label, not a headline.
feedback.wrong: Headline B is more effective: capitals (visual weight), ★ symbols (visual interest), "FUN FOR ALL THE FAMILY" sells the experience first, exclamation mark signals enthusiasm. Poster headlines must attract attention instantly.

questionJson.question: Which of these is a CALL TO ACTION?
options: "The event will take place on Saturday."
"BRING YOUR FAMILY AND FRIENDS! DON\

feedback.correct: Correct! "BRING YOUR FAMILY AND FRIENDS! DON\'T MISS OUT ON THE FUN!" is a call to action. It uses imperative verbs (Bring) and urgent language (Don\'t miss out) to tell the reader exactly what to DO. The other options provide information but do not command action.
feedback.wrong: "BRING YOUR FAMILY AND FRIENDS! DON\'T MISS OUT!" is the call to action. It uses imperative verbs (Bring) and urgency (Don\'t miss out). Calls to action tell readers what to DO, not just what IS.

questionJson.question:
Read this extract from the environmental poster:

"⚠️ 8 MILLION TONNES ⚠️
of plastic enters our oceans EVERY YEAR

Over 1 million seabirds die annually from plastic pollution"

What TWO persuasive techniques are being used?
options: Personal anecdote and formal vocabulary.
Shocking statistics to create emotional impact, and specific numbers that make the problem feel real and undeniable.
Imperative verbs and a call to action.
Rhetorical questions and a slogan.

feedback.correct: Correct! Two persuasive techniques: shocking statistics (8 million tonnes, 1 million seabirds) make the scale undeniable, and the use of specific numbers makes the problem feel real rather than vague. "Lots of plastic" is easy to dismiss. "8 million tonnes" is not.
feedback.wrong: Two techniques: shocking statistics (8 million tonnes, 1 million seabirds) make the scale of the problem undeniable, and specific numbers make it feel real. Compare "lots of plastic" (easy to dismiss) with "8 million tonnes" (impossible to dismiss).

questionJson.question:
Read this extract from the lost dog poster:

"OUR FAMILY IS HEARTBROKEN
Max is a much-loved family pet. Our two children, aged 6 and 9, are devastated. He needs daily medication for a health condition. We are very worried."

What persuasive technique is being used, and why is it effective?
options: Statistics – showing how many dogs go missing each year.
Emotional appeal – specific personal details (children\

feedback.correct: Correct! This is emotional appeal. The specific details (children aged 6 and 9, daily medication) are chosen because they create empathy. The reader imagines a real family, real children, a real health risk – and feels motivated to help. Vague emotional appeals ("we really miss him") are weaker because they feel generic.
feedback.wrong: Emotional appeal. Specific details – children\'s ages (6 and 9), daily medication – make the situation feel personal and real. Specific emotional appeals are stronger than vague ones because the reader imagines a real family, not an abstract situation.

questionJson.question:
Read these two versions of the same message from the environmental poster:

A: "You should try to use fewer plastic bottles if possible."
B: "REFUSE SINGLE-USE PLASTICS
✓ Use refillable water bottles
✓ Bring reusable shopping bags
✓ Say NO to plastic straws"

What makes Version B more persuasive?
options: Version A is more persuasive because it gives a reason.

Version B uses a bold command heading, imperative verbs (Use, Bring, Say), and a checklist format with ✓ symbols that make each action feel achievable. Version A hedges with "should try" and "if possible".
Version A is better because it is shorter.
Version B is better because it uses bullet points.

feedback.correct: Correct! Version B is stronger for three reasons: the bold command heading (REFUSE), imperative verbs (Use, Bring, Say), and the ✓ checklist format that makes each action feel specific and doable. Version A uses weak hedging ("should try", "if possible") that reduces urgency.
feedback.wrong: Version B: bold command heading (REFUSE), imperative verbs (Use, Bring, Say), ✓ checklist makes actions feel specific and achievable. Version A hedges ("should try", "if possible") which reduces urgency. Posters command; they do not suggest.

questionJson.question: Match each poster type to its most important feature.
questionJson.instruction: Match each item on the left to its pair on the right.

questionJson.question: Which combination of persuasive techniques is present in the Reading Challenge poster?
options: Statistics, shocking facts, and a warning about consequences.

Tiered rewards (creating motivation), testimonials from peers (creating social proof), imperative verbs (creating urgency), and inclusive language ("any genre", "any length") making the goal feel achievable.
Emotional appeal, lost-pet-style urgent language, and a reward.
Technical vocabulary, formal register, and an argument with evidence.

feedback.correct: Correct! The Reading Challenge uses: tiered rewards (Bronze to Platinum) to motivate, peer testimonials ("I never thought I\'d read 10 books but I read 15!") to provide social proof, imperative verbs (Join, Start, Discover) to create urgency, and inclusive language (any genre, any length, any pace) to reduce barriers.
feedback.wrong: Reading Challenge techniques: tiered rewards (motivation), peer testimonials (social proof – if Jamie can do it, so can I), imperative verbs (urgency), and inclusive language (any genre, any length) reducing barriers for reluctant readers.

questionJson.question: A designer is creating a poster for a school charity bake sale. Which information hierarchy is correct?
options: Contact email → Date → List of cakes → Headline
Headline (event name + excitement) → Date, time, and location → What is available → Price and how proceeds are used → Call to action
Full list of ingredients used → Date → Contact information → Headline
Detailed baking instructions → Date → Headline → Price

feedback.correct: Correct! Hierarchy for an event poster: Headline (grab attention) → Practical details (date, time, location – the most important information after getting attention) → What\'s there (content) → Cost and purpose → Call to action. Nobody reads the details if the headline doesn\'t attract them first.
feedback.wrong: Correct hierarchy: Headline first (attract attention), then practical details (date/time/location – the key information), then what\'s available, then price and purpose, then call to action. The most important job first.

questionJson.question: Match each persuasive technique to the example that uses it.
questionJson.instruction: Match each item on the left to its pair on the right.

questionJson.question:
A pupil has written this poster headline:

"Information about a school trip to the Science Museum next month"

How should they improve it to meet the poster standard?
options: "A school trip to the Science Museum is happening next month. Please read this poster for information."
"SCIENCE ADVENTURE AWAITS! 🔬\nJOIN US AT THE SCIENCE MUSEUM\nFriday 14th February"
"The school is going to the Science Museum and pupils may wish to attend if interested."
"IMPORTANT NOTICE: School Trip Arrangements"

feedback.correct: Correct! Option B transforms the label into a headline. "SCIENCE ADVENTURE AWAITS!" uses capitals and an exclamation mark to create excitement. The emoji adds visual interest. The event is named clearly. The date is immediately visible. Every word earns its place.
feedback.wrong: Option B is correct. It has: an exciting hook ("SCIENCE ADVENTURE AWAITS!"), visual interest (emoji, capitals), clear event name, and specific date. The original is a label; the improved version is a headline that makes people want to read more.

questionJson.question: Complete this call to action from the environmental poster with the correct imperative verb.
questionJson.template: Complete this call to action from the environmental poster with the correct imperative verb.
questionJson.feedback: Correct! "Start" is an imperative verb commanding immediate action. Other imperative verbs in the model posters: Bring, Join, Refuse, Use, Say, Call, Don\'t miss. Calls to action always open with an imperative verb and often include a time word (Today, This week, Now) to create urgency.

questionJson.question:
Read this lost dog poster contact section:

"Please call us when you can at the number below during normal business hours if you happen to see the dog."

How should this be rewritten for a poster?
options: "If at all possible, we would be very grateful if you could contact us at your earliest convenience."
"PLEASE CONTACT URGENTLY:\nMobile: 07700 900123\nAvailable 24 hours – call ANY TIME!"
"You may wish to consider contacting us if you see the dog."
"Information about the lost dog is available from the following contact details listed below."

feedback.correct: Correct! Option B uses capitals for urgency (URGENTLY), presents the number immediately and clearly (not buried in a sentence), and adds key information (24 hours, ANY TIME) that removes barriers to contact. On a poster, every sentence must be scannable in two seconds.
feedback.wrong: Option B is correct. Posters must be scannable. "PLEASE CONTACT URGENTLY" + number + "24 hours ANY TIME" – all the essential information in seconds. The original buries the call to action in a long, hedging sentence. Posters have no room for hedging.

questionJson.question: Which slogan is MOST effective for an anti-littering campaign poster?
options: "Please do not drop litter because it is harmful to the environment and expensive for councils to clear up."
"If you drop it, own it. Pick it up."
"Littering is a negative behaviour that negatively impacts communities negatively."
"Some people sometimes drop litter and this can be a problem."

feedback.correct: Correct! Option B is the strongest slogan: short (seven words), uses direct address ("you"), has a rhythmic pattern (drop/own/pick), and the second sentence is an imperative command. Slogans must be short enough to remember, rhythmic enough to stick, and direct enough to act on.
feedback.wrong: Option B is the best slogan: short (7 words), direct address ("you"), rhythmic pattern (drop/own/pick), ends with an imperative command. Slogans must be memorable, short, and direct. "If you drop it, own it. Pick it up." works on all three counts.

questionJson.question:
A pupil is writing an environmental campaign poster about saving bees. They have written:

"Bees are very important insects. They help flowers grow and make honey. Lots of bees are dying because of pesticides and habitat loss. We should try to help them if we can. You could plant some flowers."

What are the THREE main problems with this as a poster?
options: The sentences are too short, the topic is wrong, and there is no date.

No headline (nothing to grab attention), no statistics (vague claims like "lots of bees" and "very important" instead of specific numbers), and weak calls to action (hedging language: "should try", "could plant" instead of imperative commands: "Plant flowers NOW!").
The poster is too long and needs fewer words.
The sentences use past tense when they should use present tense.

feedback.correct: Correct! Three problems: (1) No headline – the reader has no reason to stop and read. (2) No statistics – "lots of bees are dying" is easily ignored; "1 in 10 European bee species faces extinction" is not. (3) Weak calls to action – "should try" and "could plant" hedge rather than command. Posters command.
feedback.wrong: Three problems: no headline (nothing to attract attention), no statistics (vague "lots of bees" vs specific numbers), and weak hedging language ("should try", "could plant" vs imperative commands "Plant! Act! Join!"). All three reduce persuasive impact.
