questionJson.question: Which of these is the BEST closing slogan for the environmental poster "Our Oceans Need Your Help"?
options: "In conclusion, plastic pollution is a very serious problem that needs to be addressed by everyone working together to make changes."
"SMALL CHANGES × MILLIONS OF PEOPLE = MASSIVE IMPACT\nBe part of the solution, not the pollution."
"Thank you for reading this poster about plastic pollution. We hope you will consider making some changes."
"Plastic is bad. Please stop using plastic. The ocean needs help. Thank you."

feedback.correct: Correct! Option B uses a formula (SMALL × MILLIONS = MASSIVE) that makes individual action feel powerful, and ends with a slogan ("solution/pollution") using word contrast for memorability. Option A is an essay conclusion. Option C is polite but weak. Option D is a list.
feedback.wrong: Option B is strongest: the formula shows how individual actions multiply into collective impact, and "solution/pollution" is a memorable wordplay slogan. Closing slogans must be short, memorable, and make the reader feel their action matters.

questionJson.question: Read this extract from the Reading Challenge poster. Use the checklist to evaluate it.
A pupil is designing a poster to advertise a school club for coding. Which approach is correct?
options: Write four paragraphs of continuous prose explaining what coding is and why it is useful, ending with a contact email.

Design a poster with: a headline ("BECOME A CODER! 💻"), key details (day/time/location), three bullet-pointed benefits, a call to action ("JOIN TODAY – SPACES LIMITED!"), and contact information on one simple poster.
Write a formal letter to parents explaining the benefits of coding and requesting their permission for their child to attend.
Create a list of all the coding languages that will be taught, in alphabetical order, with technical explanations of each one.

feedback.correct: Correct! Option B applies all poster principles: exciting headline, practical details, benefits in bullet points (scannable), urgency in the call to action ("SPACES LIMITED" creates scarcity), and contact information. A poster is not an essay, a letter, or a technical manual.
feedback.wrong: Option B is correct. Posters need: an exciting headline, practical details (day/time/location), benefits in bullet points (scannable, not prose), a call to action with urgency, and contact information. Never continuous prose, never a letter, never a technical manual.

questionJson.question: Progressive Writing Practice (PWP)
questionJson.modelAnswer: SAVE OUR SCHOOL GARDEN! 🌻 – Over 200 pupils use our growing space every week – JOIN THE VOLUNTEER TEAM and help us keep it blooming this summer!

answerJson.modelAnswer: SAVE OUR SCHOOL GARDEN! 🌻 – Over 200 pupils use our growing space every week – JOIN THE VOLUNTEER TEAM and help us keep it blooming this summer!

questionJson.question: What is the key difference between writing a speech and writing an essay or report?
options: A speech must be longer than a report and include more technical vocabulary.
A speech is written to be HEARD by a live audience, so it uses direct address ("you"), repetition, and rhetorical questions to create a spoken connection. An essay is written to be READ privately.
A speech must be in first person; an essay must be in third person.
A speech has no structure; an essay must have paragraphs.

feedback.correct: Correct! Speeches are written for the ear, not the eye. This changes everything: direct address ("you"), repetition for emphasis, rhetorical questions, and a spoken rhythm that would feel odd on a page. An essay reader can re-read a sentence; a listener cannot.
feedback.wrong: Speeches are written to be HEARD. This means direct address, repetition, rhetorical questions, and spoken rhythm. An essay is written to be READ. These different purposes produce different language choices throughout.

questionJson.question:
Read this speech opening:

"Good morning everyone. Imagine visiting any place in the world without leaving your chair. Picture yourself travelling back in time to ancient Egypt, or forward to distant planets. How? Simply by opening a book."

What THREE techniques are being used in this opening?
options: Statistics, subheadings, and a slogan.
Direct greeting ("Good morning everyone"), imperative verbs creating vivid imagination ("Imagine", "Picture"), and a rhetorical question ("How?") that is immediately answered to create surprise.
Past tense narrative, technical vocabulary, and a call to action.
Third person description, causal language, and a definition.

feedback.correct: Correct! Three techniques: direct greeting establishes the speaker-audience relationship immediately, "Imagine" and "Picture" are imperative verbs that pull the audience into a mental scene, and the single-word rhetorical question "How?" followed immediately by the answer creates a satisfying rhetorical pause.
feedback.wrong: Three techniques: direct greeting (Good morning everyone), imperative verbs pulling the audience into a vivid scene (Imagine, Picture), and a rhetorical question with immediate answer (How? Simply by opening a book). All three connect the speaker to the audience in the first 30 seconds.

questionJson.question: Which of these is a RHETORICAL QUESTION as used in speech writing?
options: "What time does the meeting start?" (asked to find out the answer)
\"Have you ever noticed how a book feels different from its film version?\" (asked to make the audience think and agree, not to get an answer from them)
"Please tell me how you solved this problem."
"I wonder if anyone knows the answer to this question."

feedback.correct: Correct! A rhetorical question is one the speaker does not expect the audience to answer out loud. It is asked to make the audience think, agree, or feel something. "Have you ever noticed how a book feels different from its film version?" invites the audience to reflect – the answer is already implied.
feedback.wrong: A rhetorical question is asked to make the audience think or agree, not to get an answer. "Have you ever noticed...?" assumes the audience has noticed and invites them to agree. The speaker continues without waiting for a response.

questionJson.question:
Read these two versions of the same speech point:

A: "People who read regularly learn faster and perform better at school because reading trains their brains to absorb information efficiently."

B: "Reading improves school performance, as evidenced by several studies."

Why is Version A more effective as a speech point?
options: Version A is longer, which makes it more persuasive.

Version A explains the mechanism (why and how reading improves performance) and uses direct second-person address context ("People"), making it feel real and immediate. Version B is vague ("several studies") and passive.
Version A uses a statistic; Version B does not.
Version A uses academic vocabulary; Version B is too simple.

feedback.correct: Correct! Version A explains the WHY (trains brains to absorb information), making the claim feel grounded and real. Version B uses vague attribution ("several studies") without explaining the mechanism. In speech writing, specific explanations are more persuasive than vague references to unnamed evidence.
feedback.wrong: Version A explains HOW and WHY (reading trains brains to absorb information efficiently), making the claim feel real. Version B hides behind "several studies" without explaining anything. Speeches win audiences with specific explanations, not vague references.

questionJson.question:
Read this extract from the motivational speech:

"Think about muscles. When you exercise, muscles develop tiny tears. As they heal, they become stronger. Your mind works similarly. When you struggle with something difficult and keep trying, your brain creates new connections and becomes more powerful."

What rhetorical technique is being used, and why is it effective?
options: Repetition – the word "stronger" is repeated to create emphasis.
Analogy – the audience understands muscles (something familiar) and is then invited to apply that understanding to the mind (something less visible). This makes an abstract idea concrete.
Rhetorical question – asking the audience to think about muscles.
Statistics – the claim about brain connections uses scientific data.

feedback.correct: Correct! This is an analogy: the speaker uses the familiar, physical experience of muscles to explain the less visible process of mental resilience. Analogies work in speeches because audiences can check the familiar idea against their own experience and trust it. Abstract ideas become concrete through comparison.
feedback.wrong: Analogy. The speaker uses something familiar (muscles strengthening through exercise) to explain something less visible (the mind strengthening through struggle). Analogies make abstract ideas concrete – the audience can feel the truth of the muscle metaphor in their own bodies and trust the comparison.

questionJson.question:
Read this conclusion from the thank-you speech:

"We notice. We appreciate you. We are grateful."

What rhetorical effect does this three-sentence structure create?
options: It ends the speech quickly so the audience can leave.

The three short sentences create a rhythm of escalating sincerity. The repetition of "we" builds collective voice. Each sentence is a complete, emphatic statement – the brevity makes each word carry maximum weight. The final sentence is the emotional peak.
The short sentences show that the speaker could not think of anything else to say.
The sentences use passive voice to maintain a formal register.

feedback.correct: Correct! Three short sentences, escalating in emotional intensity: noticing (awareness) → appreciating (value) → gratitude (feeling). The repetition of "We" builds collective voice. Brevity at the end of a speech is powerful – after a long speech, three short punchy sentences create impact through contrast. Less language, more meaning.
feedback.wrong: Three short sentences, escalating in sincerity: noticing → appreciating → gratitude. The repetition of "We" is collective voice. Brevity at the end of a speech creates impact through contrast with what came before. Every word is essential. This is the opposite of the opening hook – compression replaces expansion.

questionJson.question: Match each speech type to its primary purpose.
questionJson.instruction: Match each item on the left to its pair on the right.

questionJson.question: Sort these rhetorical devices and structural features into two groups: SPEECH FEATURES (used in speeches but not typical essays) and SHARED FEATURES (used in both speeches and essays).\n\n"Good morning everyone" | subheadings | "Have you ever noticed...?" | three-point structure | "Imagine visiting..." | direct address ("you") | formal vocabulary | repetition for emphasis
options: Speech features: subheadings, three-point structure, formal vocabulary\nShared features: direct address, rhetorical questions, repetition

Speech features: "Good morning everyone", "Have you ever noticed...?", "Imagine visiting...", direct address ("you"), repetition for emphasis\nShared features: subheadings, three-point structure, formal vocabulary
Speech features: formal vocabulary, subheadings, three-point structure\nShared features: "Good morning", rhetorical questions, direct address
All features are shared between speeches and essays.

feedback.correct: Correct! Speech-specific: greeting, rhetorical questions, imaginative imperatives ("Imagine"), direct personal address ("you"), repetition for spoken emphasis. Shared: three-point structure, formal vocabulary, subheadings (though speeches use spoken signposts like "Firstly" rather than written subheadings). The speech features all assume a live audience.
feedback.wrong: Speech-specific: greeting ("Good morning"), rhetorical questions, "Imagine" imperatives, direct address ("you"), repetition for spoken emphasis. These all assume a live audience who must be engaged differently from a private reader. Structure and vocabulary can be shared.

questionJson.question: A pupil is writing a persuasive speech about why schools should have a longer lunch break. Which OPENING is most effective?
options: "I am going to talk to you today about why I think schools should have a longer lunch break. I have three reasons for this."

"Good morning everyone. Raise your hand if you have ever felt stressed, rushed, and hungry all at once. What if I told you that 30 extra minutes every day could change all three of those feelings? Today, I want to convince you that a longer lunch break would transform our school day."
"In this speech, the topic of longer lunch breaks in schools will be discussed. There are several arguments in favour of this proposal."
"Lunch breaks are important for pupils. Some schools have longer breaks than others. I will discuss this subject today."

feedback.correct: Correct! Option B has: a direct greeting, a show-of-hands activity that immediately involves the audience, a rhetorical question, and a clear purpose statement. It engages the audience physically and emotionally before stating the subject. Options A, C, and D describe the speech rather than delivering it.
feedback.wrong: Option B is correct. It opens with direct greeting, involves the audience physically (raise your hand), uses a rhetorical question, and states the purpose with energy. Options A and D describe the speech. Option C uses passive voice and essays language ("will be discussed").

questionJson.question: Match each rhetorical technique to its definition.
questionJson.instruction: Match each item on the left to its pair on the right.

questionJson.question:
A pupil has written this speech opening:

"Today I am going to tell you about why reading is important. Reading has several benefits. These will be discussed in this speech."

What are the TWO main problems?
options: The speech is too short and needs more words.

The opening describes the speech rather than delivering it (no hook, no audience engagement), and the language is passive and impersonal ("will be discussed") rather than active and direct for a live audience.
The speech uses present tense when it should use past tense.
The speech needs more technical vocabulary.

feedback.correct: Correct! Two problems: no hook (the opening gives the audience no reason to listen), and passive essay-style language ("will be discussed") that belongs on a page rather than in a speaker\'s voice. Compare with Model 1: "Imagine visiting any place in the world without leaving your chair." That creates immediate desire to hear more.
feedback.wrong: Two problems: no hook (no reason for the audience to engage), and passive essay language ("will be discussed") rather than direct spoken language. Compare: "Today I am going to tell you" vs "Imagine visiting any place in the world without leaving your chair." Which makes you want to listen?

questionJson.question: Complete this signpost sentence from a three-point speech.
questionJson.template: Complete this signpost sentence from a three-point speech.
questionJson.feedback: Correct! "Secondly" is a spoken signpost. Speech signposts tell the audience exactly where they are in the structure without needing to look at a page. Other signposts from the model texts: Firstly, Thirdly, Finally, First, Second, Third. Always use spoken signposts to help the audience track the speech\'s argument.

questionJson.question:
Read this speech conclusion:

"So, in conclusion, I have told you about reading, pets, and challenges. I hope you found it interesting. That is the end of my speech. Thank you."

How should this be improved?
options: "And that is all I have to say about this topic. There may be other points I have not mentioned. Thank you very much for listening to me today."
"So I challenge you: choose one book this week. Read it. Discover why millions of people love reading. Your next adventure is waiting for you on a library shelf. Thank you."
"In conclusion, the main points of this speech were: reading, pets, and challenges. These three areas are all important. I hope this was useful."
"Thank you for listening to my speech. I have now finished speaking and will sit down."

feedback.correct: Correct! Option B ends with a direct challenge (imperative: choose, read, discover), a vivid specific image ("on a library shelf"), and a memorable closing line that makes the abstract feel concrete. It circles back to the main subject without summarising, and the brevity makes it powerful.
feedback.wrong: Option B is correct. It ends with: a direct challenge to the audience (imperative verbs: choose, read, discover), a specific memorable image, and a final line that makes the abstract feel real. Compare with Option A (still hedging) and Option C (flat summary). Speech conclusions must move the audience, not summarise at them.

questionJson.question: Complete this rhetorical question from the motivational speech.
questionJson.template: Complete this rhetorical question from the motivational speech.
questionJson.feedback: Correct! "Imagine if she had given up!" is a rhetorical question in the imperative-imagination form. It asks the audience to picture a world where one of the most successful authors never published. The contrast with what actually happened makes the resilience message more powerful.

questionJson.question:
A pupil is writing a persuasive speech about why their school should have a garden. They have written this body paragraph:

"Gardens are good because plants grow in them. You can grow vegetables and flowers. It is nice to be outside. Some schools have gardens already. Our school does not have one. I think we should get one."

What THREE improvements would most strengthen this paragraph?
options: Add more sentences, include a bibliography, and use longer words.

Add a clear signpost (Firstly/Secondly), replace vague claims ("gardens are good", "it is nice") with specific benefits supported by evidence or analogy, and use direct address to make the argument personal to the audience ("When you grow your own vegetables, you understand where food comes from").
Remove all personal opinions and make the paragraph more formal.
Change the tense from present to past throughout.

feedback.correct: Correct! Three improvements: (1) signpost (Firstly/Secondly makes the structure audible), (2) specific benefits instead of vague claims ("good", "nice" – compared to "pupils who work in school gardens reduce stress by 40%" or "growing food teaches you where it comes from"), and (3) direct address transforming the paragraph from description to persuasion ("when YOU grow your own vegetables").
feedback.wrong: Three improvements needed: signpost to signal position in the speech, specific evidence replacing vague claims, and direct address ("you") transforming description into personal persuasion. "Gardens are good" is easy to dismiss. "When you grow your own vegetables, you understand where food comes from" is not.

questionJson.question: Write the OPENING of a short speech on a topic of your choice. Include: a direct greeting, an opening hook (rhetorical question or imaginative imperative), and a clear statement of your speech\'s purpose.
questionJson.prompt: Write the OPENING of a short speech on a topic of your choice. Include: a direct greeting, an opening hook (rhetorical question or imaginative imperative), and a clear statement of your speech\'s purpose.
questionJson.modelAnswer: Good morning, everyone. Have you ever looked at a piece of litter on the ground and thought, "someone else will pick that up"? Every single day, millions of people make that same choice – and every single day, the problem grows. Today, I want to persuade you that each one of us has the power to make our school, our community, and our planet cleaner. It starts with one small decision.

answerJson.modelAnswer: Good morning, everyone. Have you ever looked at a piece of litter on the ground and thought, "someone else will pick that up"? Every single day, millions of people make that same choice – and every single day, the problem grows. Today, I want to persuade you that each one of us has the power to make our school, our community, and our planet cleaner. It starts with one small decision.

questionJson.question: Which is the BEST closing line for a motivational speech about overcoming challenges?
options: "And so, in conclusion, I have discussed three ways to overcome challenges, including learning from failure, building resilience, and asking for help. Thank you."
"Your challenges today are creating the successful, confident person you will become tomorrow. Thank you."
"I hope my speech about challenges has been useful and that you will think about the ideas I mentioned. That is all. Thank you."
"Challenges are difficult but important. Remember to try hard and ask for help if needed. Thank you very much."

feedback.correct: Correct! Option B is powerful because it is short, speaks directly to the audience ("you"), connects present struggle to future success, and ends with confident brevity. It does not summarise or hedge. The closing line of a motivational speech must leave the audience feeling capable, not just informed.
feedback.wrong: Option B is the strongest. It is brief, speaks directly to the audience ("your challenges", "you will become"), connects present difficulty to future success, and is memorable. No summary, no hedging. The closing line must leave the audience feeling something.

questionJson.question: Read this extract from the persuasive speech about reading. Use the checklist to evaluate it.
A pupil is planning a thank-you speech for their class teacher at the end of the year. Which approach correctly applies all speech writing principles?
options: Write five paragraphs summarising all the lessons covered during the year, using formal academic language throughout.

Open with a warm direct greeting, use a short anecdote about a specific memory (making it personal), structure appreciation around 2-3 specific contributions (not just general praise), use repetition ("thank you for...") for rhythmic emphasis, and close with a memorable final sentence expressing ongoing impact.
"Dear Miss Jones, I am writing to express my appreciation for the work you have done this year. You have been a good teacher. Yours sincerely."
Begin with a list of statistics about teaching hours worked, followed by a bibliography of sources confirming that teaching is a demanding profession.

feedback.correct: Correct! Option B applies all four speech principles to a thank-you speech: specific anecdote (personal, emotional connection), structured appreciation (not just general praise), repetition ("thank you for...") creating spoken rhythm, and a memorable close. Options A and C are written for the eye not the ear. Option D is an argument, not a thank-you.
feedback.wrong: Option B is correct: specific anecdote, structured appreciation, spoken repetition for rhythm, memorable close. This is a thank-you speech, not a letter (no "Dear", no "Yours sincerely"), not an essay (no bibliography), and not an argument (no statistics defending teaching).

questionJson.question: Progressive Writing Practice (PWP)
questionJson.modelAnswer: Good morning everyone. Think about the last time you learned something new, something that surprised you and changed the way you see the world – that is the feeling that a school library gives you every single day, which is exactly why I am asking you to sign our petition to save it before Friday.

answerJson.modelAnswer: Good morning everyone. Think about the last time you learned something new, something that surprised you and changed the way you see the world – that is the feeling that a school library gives you every single day, which is exactly why I am asking you to sign our petition to save it before Friday.

questionJson.question: What does "show don\'t tell" mean in descriptive writing?
options: Always use long sentences and lots of adjectives to describe everything in detail.

Instead of STATING a feeling or quality directly ("She was nervous"), you CREATE the feeling through actions, sensory details, and behaviour so the reader experiences it rather than being told about it.
Only describe what a character looks like – their hair colour, eye colour, and height.
Use as many similes as possible in every paragraph.

feedback.correct: Correct! Show don\'t tell means creating the experience for the reader rather than labelling it. Compare: "He was nervous" (telling) with "His hand left a damp print on the door handle" (showing). The reader feels the nervousness rather than being told it exists.
feedback.wrong: "Show don\'t tell" means creating the experience through details rather than labelling it. "She was angry" tells. "She slammed the drawer so hard the teacups rattled" shows. The reader feels the anger without being told what to call it.

questionJson.question:
Read this sentence:

"His fingers – long, brown, precise – moved across the face of a dismantled clock without any hesitation, as if they could read the metal by touch alone."

What does this sentence show about the clockmaker?
options: He is a clockmaker who has repaired many clocks in his life.
His extreme skill and confidence – the word "precise", the absence of hesitation, and the simile "as if they could read the metal" all show mastery without the writer ever using the word "skilled".
He has long fingers, which is unusual for a clockmaker.
He is currently repairing a clock in his shop.

feedback.correct: Correct! The writer never says "he was skilled" or "he was experienced." Instead: "precise" describes the fingers themselves; "without any hesitation" shows confidence through absence of doubt; "as if they could read the metal" is a simile that implies the fingers have almost a separate intelligence. The reader concludes mastery without being told it.
feedback.wrong: "Precise" modifies the fingers, not the clockmaker directly. "Without any hesitation" shows confidence through the absence of doubt. "As if they could read the metal by touch alone" is a simile suggesting supernatural familiarity. Together they show mastery without ever naming it.

questionJson.question: Which of these is a SETTING description?
options: "She was small, with dark eyes that missed nothing and hands that were always moving."
"He stood up before he had decided to stand up, which was its own kind of answer."

"The mud stretched out from the bank in ridged, dark sheets – not the grey mud of rain puddles but something older, heavier, carrying in its surface the pressed imprint of every tide that had ever drawn back from it."
"I wonder if she saw me. She must have. Everyone always sees."

feedback.correct: Correct! Option C describes a physical place using sensory detail (sight: ridged dark mud; age: "something older, heavier") and figurative language (the mud carries the "pressed imprint of every tide"). Options A and B describe a character and action. Option D is internal monologue – a character\'s thoughts.
feedback.wrong: Option C is a setting description: it describes a place (tidal mudflats) using sensory detail and figurative language. Option A describes a character. Option B shows a character\'s action revealing emotion. Option D is an internal thought – the character\'s voice inside their own head.

questionJson.question:
Read this extract:

"Somewhere in the reeds, something small moved with a rapid, papery sound. Oystercatchers, at a distance, called to one another in their short, sharp, certain voices. And over all of it – a silence that was not really silence but the absence of human noise, which is different."

Which SENSE is being primarily used here, and what is the most sophisticated observation in the passage?
options: Sight. The most sophisticated observation is identifying the oystercatchers by name.

Sound. The most sophisticated observation is the distinction between silence and "the absence of human noise" – noticing that a natural soundscape can feel silent to a human but is full of animal life.
Smell. The most sophisticated observation is describing the birds\

feedback.correct: Correct! This paragraph is built entirely from sound. The most sophisticated observation is the distinction between silence and "the absence of human noise" – the estuary is not quiet, it is simply empty of the specific sounds that humans make. This is the kind of precise distinction that turns observation into literature.
feedback.wrong: Sound dominates this paragraph: papery movement, oystercatcher calls, and the distinguished definition of silence. The most sophisticated line is "a silence that was not really silence but the absence of human noise" – it creates a category distinction (natural sound vs. human sound) that makes the setting feel observed rather than imagined.

questionJson.question:
Read these two versions of the same moment:

A: "Jamil was anxious in the waiting room. He felt worried about what was happening inside. Time seemed to pass slowly."

B: "The clock on the wall said 11:47. He was almost certain it had said 11:47 a few minutes ago, which meant either time had stopped or he had lost the ability to tell the difference between four minutes and forty. Both felt plausible."

What makes Version B more effective as internal monologue?
options: Version B is longer and contains more information.
Version A names the feelings directly; Version B never names them. Instead it shows the mind\

feedback.correct: Correct! Version A labels feelings ("anxious", "worried") and states the experience. Version B performs the anxious mind rather than describing it: the repetition of 11:47, the two absurd explanations for the discrepancy, the dry acceptance of both. The reader diagnoses the anxiety themselves, which is far more powerful than being told it.
feedback.wrong: Version A labels feelings and states observations. Version B performs the anxiety through the mind\'s specific behaviour: noticing the time twice, generating two absurd explanations, accepting them drily. "Both felt plausible" is not a logical response – it is a stressed mind\'s response. The reader recognises the feeling because the behaviour is so precise.

questionJson.question:
Read this sentence from the garden passage:

"She had learned, across more years than she cared to count, that a garden responds to attention the same way a person does: not to urgency, but to steadiness."

What technique is being used, and what does it reveal?
options: A simile comparing garden and person. It reveals that the character is patient.

An analogy between garden and person. It reveals her age ("more years than she cared to count"), her philosophy of care, and – because a person uses this insight when dealing with both gardens AND people – implies she has tended both with the same patience over a long life. The character is built in one sentence without a single physical description.
A metaphor. It reveals that she prefers gardening to dealing with people.
Personification, giving the garden human qualities of preference.

feedback.correct: Correct! One sentence does multiple things: "more years than she cared to count" implies age without stating it; the garden/person analogy reveals her philosophy; the specific contrast (not urgency, but steadiness) tells us how she has lived, not just how she gardens. This is how skilled fiction writers build character – through implication, not description.
feedback.wrong: The analogy builds character in one sentence: age is implied ("more years than she cared to count"), philosophy is stated (steadiness not urgency), and the comparison between garden and person suggests she has applied this wisdom to both throughout a long life. Physical description of the character is unnecessary – this single sentence shows who she is.

questionJson.question: Match each description type to its defining feature.
questionJson.instruction: Match each item on the left to its pair on the right.

questionJson.question: Five senses appear in the estuary passage. Which sentence uses TASTE (or the sense closest to taste)?
options: "The mud stretched out from the bank in ridged, dark sheets."
"Somewhere in the reeds, something small moved with a rapid, papery sound."
"In winter it sharpened to something that felt almost like a flavour – the sort that settles at the back of your throat and stays there for hours."
"The creak of a single moored boat, rope taut and complaining against the post."

feedback.correct: Correct! Option C describes how the cold mineral smell of the estuary in winter crosses into almost-taste territory: a sensation felt at the back of the throat. This is sophisticated sensory writing – noting where one sense bleeds into another. The estuary passage demonstrates all five senses: sight (ridged mud), smell (salt and mineral), sound (birds and boat), touch (cold in layers), and this near-taste.
feedback.wrong: Option C: "something that felt almost like a flavour – the sort that settles at the back of your throat" crosses from smell into the sensation closest to taste. This is an advanced technique: noticing where one sense bleeds into another rather than treating the five senses as entirely separate categories.

questionJson.question: Which of these demonstrates the MOST advanced use of "show don\'t tell"?
options: "She was very sad after the news."
"She felt overwhelmed and could not stop crying."
"She stood at the kitchen window for a long time after the call ended. The tap was dripping. She had been meaning to fix that for months."
"Her eyes were red from crying and she looked very upset."

feedback.correct: Correct! Option C never mentions sadness or emotion at all. Yet it is the most emotionally powerful: the long silence at the window, and then the mind fixing on a small domestic fact that has nothing to do with the crisis – this is exactly how shock and grief actually work. The tap dripping is a form of dissociation. The reader feels the emotion without a single emotional word.
feedback.wrong: Option C is most advanced: no emotional word appears. The long stillness, and then the mind noticing the dripping tap (which has nothing to do with the crisis), shows how shock or grief actually works – the mind detaches to something small and manageable. This is more accurate to experience than "she felt overwhelmed," and therefore more powerful.

questionJson.question: Match each figurative technique to the example from the model texts.
questionJson.instruction: Match each item on the left to its pair on the right.

questionJson.question:
A pupil has written:

"The old man was very experienced at his job and knew exactly what he was doing. He was also kind and thoughtful."

Which rewrite is the BEST example of showing rather than telling?
options: "The old man was an extremely experienced professional with many years of practice in his field."

"His fingers moved across the mechanism without hesitation. When she told him the watch had belonged to her grandfather, he set down his tools and held it to the light before speaking – not in a hurry to give an answer, but as if the watch deserved consideration first."
"He was old and had worked there for a very long time. He was nice to customers."
"The experienced and kind man worked carefully and thoughtfully on the broken clock mechanism."

feedback.correct: Correct! Option B shows experience through the hands moving without hesitation and kindness through the specific action of setting down tools, holding the watch to the light, taking time – giving the watch consideration before answering. Not a single quality is named. Every quality is demonstrated through behaviour.
feedback.wrong: Option B shows both qualities through specific behaviour: experience shown by hands moving without hesitation; kindness shown by setting tools down, holding the watch to the light, not rushing the answer. "Kind" and "experienced" never appear. The behaviour creates them.

questionJson.question: Complete this sentence from the estuary description with the correct word.
questionJson.template: Complete this sentence from the estuary description with the correct word.
questionJson.feedback: Correct! "Silence" is the word that makes the distinction powerful: the writer names it, then immediately qualifies and redefines it. This is a common technique in literary description: introduce a word, then challenge your own use of it to be more precise.

questionJson.question:
Read this opening:

"The garden in November was a particular kind of beautiful, the kind that requires some patience to see."

What does this sentence do that a simple description would not?
options: It describes the garden in November, which is a cold month.
It immediately establishes the beekeeper\

feedback.correct: Correct! This sentence does three things: it characterises November beauty (not obvious, requiring patience); it characterises the observer (someone who has the patience to see it); and it sets an expectation for the reader (look carefully at what follows). All in one sentence, before the beekeeper has even appeared.
feedback.wrong: "A particular kind of beautiful, the kind that requires some patience to see" characterises November beauty (not immediate), the observer (patient, attentive), and the reader\'s task (look carefully). This is an efficient opening sentence that does more work than it appears to.

questionJson.question: Complete this internal monologue sentence with the most effective word.
questionJson.template: Complete this internal monologue sentence with the most effective word.
questionJson.feedback: Correct! "Had" – the past perfect tense – signals a shift further back in time (a memory inside a present moment). The movement from "now" (overgrown garden, present situation) to "had been" (childhood memory) is the mind doing what stressed minds do: retreating to a safer past. The tense marks the retreat.

questionJson.question:
A pupil is writing a character description and has written:

"The teacher was strict but fair. She had grey hair and wore glasses. She was good at her job and the pupils liked her even though they were a bit scared of her sometimes."

What are the THREE main problems with this description?
options: The sentences are too short, the vocabulary is too simple, and there is no punctuation.

Physical details are minimal and generic (grey hair, glasses tell us nothing specific), all qualities are stated rather than shown ("strict but fair", "good at her job", "liked her"), and there are no actions, objects, or dialogue to build an individual person rather than a category.
The description is too short and needs to be longer.
The character has not been named, which makes it impossible to describe her.

feedback.correct: Correct! Three problems: (1) generic physical details (grey hair and glasses describe thousands of teachers), (2) all qualities stated not shown ("strict but fair", "liked her", "good at her job"), (3) no specific actions, objects, or moments that make this teacher distinct rather than a generic type. "She marked every piece of work the same day it was handed in, always in purple ink" would tell us more than "she was good at her job."
feedback.wrong: Three problems: generic physical detail (grey hair/glasses = no individual), all qualities stated (strict, fair, liked, good at job = no showing), no specific actions or objects (a character without specific behaviour is a type, not a person). The fix: find the detail that belongs only to this teacher, not all teachers.

questionJson.question: Write a SHORT setting description (4-6 sentences) of a place that creates a specific atmosphere WITHOUT naming what the atmosphere is.
questionJson.prompt: Write a SHORT setting description (4-6 sentences) of a place that creates a specific atmosphere WITHOUT naming what the atmosphere is.
questionJson.modelAnswer: The library\'s central heating ticked and settled, the kind of sound a building makes when it is slowly exhaling. The last few readers sat at separate tables, their pages turning at different intervals, each in their own silence. Near the fire door, a trolley of returned books waited to be reshelved, their spines dusty and patient. Through the tall windows, the car park lights were coming on one by one in the early dark.

answerJson.modelAnswer: The library\'s central heating ticked and settled, the kind of sound a building makes when it is slowly exhaling. The last few readers sat at separate tables, their pages turning at different intervals, each in their own silence. Near the fire door, a trolley of returned books waited to be reshelved, their spines dusty and patient. Through the tall windows, the car park lights were coming on one by one in the early dark.

questionJson.question: Which of these is the BEST example of internal monologue?
options: "Amara thought about how nervous she was feeling. She wondered what would happen next. She hoped everything would be all right."
"She imagined many different scenarios and her mind was working overtime as she tried to process all the possibilities."

"It would probably be fine. Almost certainly fine. There was no good reason for it to be anything other than completely fine. She told herself this three times, which she recognised was probably one repetition too many."
"Amara was an anxious person who always worried about things before they happened."

feedback.correct: Correct! Option C performs anxiety without naming it. The repetition of "fine" three times, and then the dry observation ("one repetition too many") shows a mind trying to reassure itself while also observing its own attempt. This is the double-consciousness of internal monologue: the self, and the self watching the self.
feedback.wrong: Option C performs anxiety through the repetition of "fine" three times – the very repetition reveals the anxiety it is trying to suppress. Then "which she recognised was probably one repetition too many" shows the mind watching itself. This double-consciousness (self + self watching self) is the hallmark of effective internal monologue.

questionJson.question:
Read the final lines of the garden passage:

"The sky was the colour of pewter. The bare apple tree moved slightly in the wind. Somewhere down the lane a dog barked twice and fell silent.

She straightened, pulled her coat closed, and went back inside to make tea."

Why is this ending effective?
options: It describes the weather and the character\

s return to ordinary life through three small sequential actions (straightened, pulled coat, went inside). The action of making tea does not resolve the emotion of the passage – instead it returns the character to daily life while the reader still holds the emotion. This is the literary technique of the "open close": the scene ends, but the feeling does not.
It ends with a simple domestic action to show that the character is practical.
The dog barking twice adds an unexpected sound to the silence.

feedback.correct: Correct! Three short observational sentences create stillness. Then three sequential actions return the character to ordinary life. "Making tea" is a domestic act so specific and British that it carries cultural weight about how people continue after quiet revelation. The passage does not explain what the beekeeper has felt. It simply ends. The emotion the reader is carrying does not resolve – it is handed to the reader to hold.
feedback.wrong: The stillness of three short observation sentences, then three small sequential actions. "Making tea" is both ordinary and weighted – it is what people do when they need to do something small after something large. The emotion is not resolved or named. The scene ends; the feeling continues. This is the "open close" technique.

questionJson.question:
A pupil wants to write a paragraph that combines character, setting, and internal thought. They have planned:

- Character: an elderly man waiting for his granddaughter to visit
- Setting: a small front room in winter
- Thought: he is worried she might not come

Which approach is correct?
options: Write three separate paragraphs: one describing the man, one describing the room, one describing his thoughts.

Open with the setting detail (the room telling us about him), weave his action through it (checking the clock, adjusting a photograph, moving to the window), let one brief internal thought appear mid-passage without explanation, and close on a sensory detail that carries the emotion without naming it.
Describe the man first, then the room, then write "He thought to himself:" and explain his worries.
Begin with a statement of the emotion ("He was worried she would not come") and then explain why he was worried using setting details.

feedback.correct: Correct! Option B weaves all three together rather than separating them into blocks. The setting details reveal character (a room tells us about the person who lives in it). The actions (checking clock, adjusting photograph) show anxiety without naming it. One brief thought appears and passes – not a block of explanation but a flash. The closing sensory detail holds the emotion for the reader.
feedback.wrong: Option B: setting reveals character (objects and arrangement of a room show the person who created it), actions show anxiety (checking clock, adjusting photograph), one brief thought appears and passes without explanation, closing sensory detail carries the emotion. Weaving is always more effective than separating into blocks.

questionJson.question: Progressive Writing Practice (PWP)
questionJson.modelAnswer: The last candle guttered and spat as Joel pulled his coat tighter around his shoulders, listening to the house settle into its nighttime sounds, wondering whether silence was something a building made or something it simply allowed.
